exe.dev

> New issues and PRs from new contributors are auto-closed by default. Maintainers review auto-closed issues daily. See [CONTRIBUTING.md](../../CONTRIBUTING.md). --- Pi is a minimal terminal coding harness. Adapt pi to your workflows, not the other way around, without having to fork and modify pi internals. Extend it with TypeScript [Extensions](#extensions), [Skills](#skills), [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates), and [Themes](#themes). Put your extensions, skills, prompt templates, and themes in [Pi Packages](#pi-packages) and share them with others via npm or git. Pi ships with powerful defaults but skips features like sub agents and plan mode. Instead, you can ask pi to build what you want or install a third party pi package that matches your workflow. Pi runs in four modes: interactive, print or JSON, RPC for process integration, and an SDK for embedding in your own apps. See [openclaw/openclaw](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw) for a real-world SDK integration. ## Share your OSS coding agent sessions If you use pi for open source work, please share your coding agent sessions. Public OSS session data helps improve models, prompts, tools, and evaluations using real development workflows. For the full explanation, see [this post on X](https://x.com/badlogicgames/status/2037811643774652911). To publish sessions, use [`badlogic/pi-share-hf`](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-share-hf). Read its README.md for setup instructions. All you need is a Hugging Face account, the Hugging Face CLI, and `pi-share-hf`. You can also watch [this video](https://x.com/badlogicgames/status/2041151967695634619), where I show how I publish my `pi-mono` sessions. I regularly publish my own `pi-mono` work sessions here: - [badlogicgames/pi-mono on Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/datasets/badlogicgames/pi-mono) ## Table of Contents - [Quick Start](#quick-start) - [Providers & Models](#providers--models) - [Interactive Mode](#interactive-mode) - [Editor](#editor) - [Commands](#commands) - [Keyboard Shortcuts](#keyboard-shortcuts) - [Message Queue](#message-queue) - [Sessions](#sessions) - [Branching](#branching) - [Compaction](#compaction) - [Settings](#settings) - [Context Files](#context-files) - [Customization](#customization) - [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates) - [Skills](#skills) - [Extensions](#extensions) - [Themes](#themes) - [Pi Packages](#pi-packages) - [Programmatic Usage](#programmatic-usage) - [Philosophy](#philosophy) - [CLI Reference](#cli-reference) --- ## Quick Start ```bash npm install -g @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent ``` Authenticate with an API key: ```bash export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-... pi ``` Or use your existing subscription: ```bash pi /login # Then select provider ``` Then just talk to pi. By default, pi gives the model four tools: `read`, `write`, `edit`, and `bash`. The model uses these to fulfill your requests. Add capabilities via [skills](#skills), [prompt templates](#prompt-templates), [extensions](#extensions), or [pi packages](#pi-packages). **Platform notes:** [Windows](docs/windows.md) | [Termux (Android)](docs/termux.md) | [tmux](docs/tmux.md) | [Terminal setup](docs/terminal-setup.md) | [Shell aliases](docs/shell-aliases.md) --- ## Providers & Models For each built-in provider, pi maintains a list of tool-capable models, updated with every release. Authenticate via subscription (`/login`) or API key, then select any model from that provider via `/model` (or Ctrl+L). **Subscriptions:** - Anthropic Claude Pro/Max - OpenAI ChatGPT Plus/Pro (Codex) - GitHub Copilot - Google Gemini CLI - Google Antigravity **API keys:** - Anthropic - OpenAI - Azure OpenAI - Google Gemini - Google Vertex - Amazon Bedrock - Mistral - Groq - Cerebras - xAI - OpenRouter - Vercel AI Gateway - ZAI - OpenCode Zen - OpenCode Go - Hugging Face - Kimi For Coding - MiniMax See [docs/providers.md](docs/providers.md) for detailed setup instructions. **Custom providers & models:** Add providers via `~/.pi/agent/models.json` if they speak a supported API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google). For custom APIs or OAuth, use extensions. See [docs/models.md](docs/models.md) and [docs/custom-provider.md](docs/custom-provider.md). --- ## Interactive Mode

The interface from top to bottom: - **Startup header** - Shows shortcuts (`/hotkeys` for all), loaded AGENTS.md files, prompt templates, skills, and extensions - **Messages** - Your messages, assistant responses, tool calls and results, notifications, errors, and extension UI - **Editor** - Where you type; border color indicates thinking level - **Footer** - Working directory, session name, total token/cache usage, cost, context usage, current model The editor can be temporarily replaced by other UI, like built-in `/settings` or custom UI from extensions (e.g., a Q&A tool that lets the user answer model questions in a structured format). [Extensions](#extensions) can also replace the editor, add widgets above/below it, a status line, custom footer, or overlays. ### Editor | Feature | How | |---------|-----| | File reference | Type `@` to fuzzy-search project files | | Path completion | Tab to complete paths | | Multi-line | Shift+Enter (or Ctrl+Enter on Windows Terminal) | | Images | Ctrl+V to paste (Alt+V on Windows), or drag onto terminal | | Bash commands | `!command` runs and sends output to LLM, `!!command` runs without sending | Standard editing keybindings for delete word, undo, etc. See [docs/keybindings.md](docs/keybindings.md). ### Commands Type `/` in the editor to trigger commands. [Extensions](#extensions) can register custom commands, [skills](#skills) are available as `/skill:name`, and [prompt templates](#prompt-templates) expand via `/templatename`. | Command | Description | |---------|-------------| | `/login`, `/logout` | OAuth authentication | | `/model` | Switch models | | `/scoped-models` | Enable/disable models for Ctrl+P cycling | | `/settings` | Thinking level, theme, message delivery, transport | | `/resume` | Pick from previous sessions | | `/new` | Start a new session | | `/name ` | Set session display name | | `/session` | Show session info (file, ID, messages, tokens, cost) | | `/tree` | Jump to any point in the session and continue from there | | `/fork` | Create a new session from a previous user message | | `/clone` | Duplicate the current active branch into a new session | | `/compact [prompt]` | Manually compact context, optional custom instructions | | `/copy` | Copy last assistant message to clipboard | | `/export [file]` | Export session to HTML file | | `/share` | Upload as private GitHub gist with shareable HTML link | | `/reload` | Reload keybindings, extensions, skills, prompts, and context files (themes hot-reload automatically) | | `/hotkeys` | Show all keyboard shortcuts | | `/changelog` | Display version history | | `/quit` | Quit pi | ### Keyboard Shortcuts See `/hotkeys` for the full list. Customize via `~/.pi/agent/keybindings.json`. See [docs/keybindings.md](docs/keybindings.md). **Commonly used:** | Key | Action | |-----|--------| | Ctrl+C | Clear editor | | Ctrl+C twice | Quit | | Escape | Cancel/abort | | Escape twice | Open `/tree` | | Ctrl+L | Open model selector | | Ctrl+P / Shift+Ctrl+P | Cycle scoped models forward/backward | | Shift+Tab | Cycle thinking level | | Ctrl+O | Collapse/expand tool output | | Ctrl+T | Collapse/expand thinking blocks | ### Message Queue Submit messages while the agent is working: - **Enter** queues a *steering* message, delivered after the current assistant turn finishes executing its tool calls - **Alt+Enter** queues a *follow-up* message, delivered only after the agent finishes all work - **Escape** aborts and restores queued messages to editor - **Alt+Up** retrieves queued messages back to editor On Windows Terminal, `Alt+Enter` is fullscreen by default. Remap it in [docs/terminal-setup.md](docs/terminal-setup.md) so pi can receive the follow-up shortcut. Configure delivery in [settings](docs/settings.md): `steeringMode` and `followUpMode` can be `"one-at-a-time"` (default, waits for response) or `"all"` (delivers all queued at once). `transport` selects provider transport preference (`"sse"`, `"websocket"`, or `"auto"`) for providers that support multiple transports. --- ## Sessions Sessions are stored as JSONL files with a tree structure. Each entry has an `id` and `parentId`, enabling in-place branching without creating new files. See [docs/session.md](docs/session.md) for file format. ### Management Sessions auto-save to `~/.pi/agent/sessions/` organized by working directory. ```bash pi -c # Continue most recent session pi -r # Browse and select from past sessions pi --no-session # Ephemeral mode (don't save) pi --session # Use specific session file or ID pi --fork # Fork specific session file or ID into a new session ``` Use `/session` in interactive mode to see the current session ID before reusing it with `--session ` or `--fork `. ### Branching **`/tree`** - Navigate the session tree in-place. Select any previous point, continue from there, and switch between branches. All history preserved in a single file.

- Search by typing, fold/unfold and jump between branches with Ctrl+←/Ctrl+→ or Alt+←/Alt+→, page with ←/→ - Filter modes (Ctrl+O): default → no-tools → user-only → labeled-only → all - Press Shift+L to label entries as bookmarks and Shift+T to toggle label timestamps **`/fork`** - Create a new session file from a previous user message on the active branch. Opens a selector, copies the active path up to that point, and places the selected prompt in the editor for modification. **`/clone`** - Duplicate the current active branch into a new session file at the current position. The new session keeps the full active-path history and opens with an empty editor. **`--fork `** - Fork an existing session file or partial session UUID directly from the CLI. This copies the full source session into a new session file in the current project. ### Compaction Long sessions can exhaust context windows. Compaction summarizes older messages while keeping recent ones. **Manual:** `/compact` or `/compact ` **Automatic:** Enabled by default. Triggers on context overflow (recovers and retries) or when approaching the limit (proactive). Configure via `/settings` or `settings.json`. Compaction is lossy. The full history remains in the JSONL file; use `/tree` to revisit. Customize compaction behavior via [extensions](#extensions). See [docs/compaction.md](docs/compaction.md) for internals. --- [364 more lines in file. Use offset=261 to continue.]

**Outlining a custom tool's function**

The output would be in JSON format, detailing implementation loci, spec clauses, an existing test inventory, and categorized proposed new tests like `spec-positive` and `spec-negative`. It might also offer a duplicate score and allowed targets. I realize skills are easier to create, so I could describe a skill structure or extension architecture instead, possibly including a prompt template.

**Creating a prompt template for audits**

I’m considering including a prompt template called `type-alias-spec-audit.md`. This would have a description focused on adding non-duplicated, spec-conformant ETS CTS tests for a language feature. It would emphasize auditing existing coverage before mirroring anything into the release suite. 

In the task section, it would outline steps like reading the nearest AGENTS and spec docs, locating the frontend implementation, inventorying existing tests in ets-templates and ets-cts-7.0, and building a delta matrix with key columns for organization.

content.text:
Yes. The main reason this took many prompts is that the task actually had **three different outputs** mixed together:

1. **green CTS tests**  
   - must be **spec-conformant**
   - must be **non-duplicated**
   - must land in `ets-templates` and then in `ets-cts-7.0`

2. **non-conformance findings**  
   - useful for frontend/runtime debugging
   - but should **not** go into green CTS unless the test is spec-conformant

3. **crash repros**  
   - useful for frontend/runtime robustness
   - usually should live as **separate repros**, not in CTS, unless they are also direct spec tests

We spent prompts disentangling those.

---

# What slowed this session down

## 1. The acceptance criteria were not fixed upfront
The biggest late correction was:

> “All added tests should be spec-conformant, not capture current behavior”

If that had been explicit in the first request, I would have skipped:
- characterization tests
- “difference” CTS tests that pass on current behavior
- crash-oriented CTS tests based on unsupported TS syntax

That alone would have saved a lot of prompts and edits.

---

## 2. Deduplication scope was too narrow at first
Initially, dedup was effectively against the immediate type-alias folder.

But the real duplicate surface was broader:
- `04.names.../01.type_alias_declaration`
- **and** `05.generics/.../generic_type_alias`
- **and** existing `spec*.ets` tests in the same type-alias chapter

So some “new” tests were not really new.

If you had required:

> deduplicate not only against the same folder, but also neighboring generic-type-alias tests and existing spec examples

that would have prevented several later removals.

---

## 3. Porting to `ets-cts-7.0` happened too early
The copy to release suite should happen **last**, after:
- spec audit
- dedup audit
- pruning

Instead, we copied and then had to clean both trees.

---

## 4. Naming constraints came late
You later required:
- names by spec feature/sub-feature
- no “impl”, “difference”, “regression”

That was a good rule, but it came after files already existed.

---

## 5. `32651.ets` was introduced after the first wave
This added an extra subtask:
- locate frontend test
- port it
- then parameterize it

If it had been part of the original contract, it would have been folded into the initial plan.

---

# How to get the same result faster next time

## Best user strategy
Give the agent a **strict staged contract** in one prompt.

Here is the key structure:

### A. Define the allowed artifact types
Tell the agent up front:

- Add **only** spec-conformant, green CTS tests to:
  - `ets-templates`
  - and then mirror to `ets-cts-7.0`
- Do **not** add characterization tests
- Do **not** add current-behavior tests
- Do **not** add crash-only repros to CTS
- If non-conformance or crash repros are found, report them separately

That single instruction would have removed the biggest branch of wasted work.

---

## B. Force dedup before edits
Ask for this exact rule:

- Audit existing tests in:
  - target feature folder
  - neighboring spec chapter tests
  - generic-type-alias tests in `05.generics`
- Add **only delta coverage**
- Treat “very close” as duplicate, not just exact duplication

That would have prevented later pruning.

---

## C. Make porting the last step
Say:

- First finalize the unique tests in `ets-templates`
- Only after dedup is complete, mirror the final set into `ets-cts-7.0`

That avoids double work.

---

## D. Require a test matrix before edits
Even if you want fewer prompts, you can still ask for this **inside one prompt**:

> Before editing, produce a compact matrix:
> spec clause | implementation locus | closest existing test | add/reuse/skip | reason

Then continue and edit only the “add” rows.

This costs some tool calls, but saves many later turns.

---

# A better one-shot prompt

Use something like this next time:

```text
Feature: Type Aliases.

Goal:
Create only NEW, NON-DUPLICATED, SPEC-CONFORMANT tests for ets-templates, then mirror the FINAL set into ets-cts-7.0.

Hard rules:
1. All added CTS tests must be spec-conformant.
2. Do NOT add characterization/current-behavior tests.
3. Do NOT add crash-only or implementation-specific repros to CTS.
4. If you find frontend/runtime non-conformance or crash repros, report them separately, but do not add them to CTS unless they are direct spec tests.
5. Deduplicate not only against the target folder, but also against neighboring relevant folders, especially generic type alias tests in 05.generics.
6. Treat “very close” tests as duplicates and do not add them.
7. Name new files by spec feature/sub-feature, not by implementation, regression, crash, or difference wording.
8. Do not touch existing frontend tests.
9. Port frontend test /path/to/32651.ets into CTS only if the resulting CTS test is spec-conformant and non-duplicated.
10. Mirror to ets-cts-7.0 only after the final deduplicated set is ready.

Process:
- First locate implementation and relevant spec clauses.
- Then inventory existing tests and build a delta matrix:
  spec clause | implementation locus | closest existing test | add/reuse/skip | reason.
- Then add only the delta tests.
- Then mirror the final set into ets-cts-7.0.
- Then list:
  a) final added CTS files
  b) skipped duplicates
  c) separate non-conformance/crash findings not added to CTS.
```

That would likely have cut this down a lot.

---

# How to reduce tool calls specifically

## Ask for “chapter-aware dedup”
If you say only:
- “check existing tests”

the agent may search too locally.

If you say:
- “dedup against the same chapter and neighboring chapter(s) that semantically own generic alias behavior”

it can target fewer searches with better yield.

---

## Ask for “final-only mirroring”
This avoids:
- duplicate file writes
- duplicate renames
- duplicate pruning in both trees

---

## Separate “report” from “edit”
The efficient split is:

### Phase 1
- spec + implementation + existing test audit
- no edits yet

### Phase 2
- edit only approved unique CTS delta

Even though this sounds like two phases, it often uses **fewer total tool calls** than editing, renaming, pruning, copying, and re-pruning.

---

# What I would do differently next time
If starting over, I would use this internal workflow:

1. locate implementation
2. locate spec clauses
3. inventory existing tests in:
   - type alias chapter
   - spec examples
   - generic type alias chapter
4. classify candidate tests as:
   - spec-positive CTS
   - spec-negative CTS
   - non-conformance repro only
   - crash repro only
5. edit only the CTS delta
6. mirror only at the end

That is the shortest clean path.

---

# Best pi idea: an extension

## Best fit
For this workflow, the **best long-term fit is a pi extension**, not just a prompt template.

Why:
- duplicate detection needs automation
- chapter-aware test inventory needs automation
- mirroring to release suite needs automation
- distinguishing CTS-safe vs repro-only tests needs automation

A prompt template helps, but it won’t reduce tool churn nearly as much.

---

## Proposed extension: `cts-delta-auditor`

A pi extension could add a command and custom tools for exactly this workflow.

### Custom command
- `/cts-audit <feature> [frontend-test]`

### Custom tools
#### 1. `scan_feature_tests`
Inputs:
- feature name
- spec paths
- implementation paths
- suite roots

Outputs:
- relevant implementation loci
- relevant spec clauses
- existing test inventory

#### 2. `classify_test_candidate`
Inputs:
- candidate code
- spec clause
- existing test inventory

Outputs:
- `spec_positive_cts`
- `spec_negative_cts`
- `nonconformance_repro`
- `crash_repro`
- duplicate score
- nearest existing test

#### 3. `mirror_cts_delta`
Inputs:
- final approved files
- source suite root
- release suite root

Outputs:
- copy/sync result

---

## Key feature: semantic duplicate fingerprint
The extension should fingerprint tests by semantics, not text.

Example fingerprint fields:
- feature chapter
- positive/negative
- alias RHS kind:
  - primitive
  - array
  - function
  - tuple
  - generic class reference
  - generic interface reference
  - union
- generic arity condition:
  - omitted
  - exact
  - too many
- recursion context:
  - array element
  - generic type argument
  - function param
  - tuple element
  - union member
- constraint kind:
  - primitive
  - class
  - interface
- expected diagnostic code

That would have automatically flagged most of the duplicates we removed later.

---

## Another useful guard
The extension could block writes to `ets-cts-7.0` until:
- duplicate audit is complete
- candidate set is marked `final`

That would prevent premature mirroring.

This is a good match for pi extensions because extensions can:
- register tools
- register commands
- intercept tool calls
- persist session state

all documented in the pi extension model.

---

# Lower-effort alternative: a skill

If you want something lighter than an extension, a **skill** is a good second-best option.

## Skill idea
`spec-conformant-cts`

It would contain:
- a fixed workflow
- helper shell/python scripts
- a checklist
- a delta-matrix format

### What the skill would enforce
- read spec first
- inventory existing tests in same and neighboring chapters
- classify findings into CTS-safe vs repro-only
- mirror only after final dedup

This would reduce prompt ambiguity, but not reduce tool calls as much as an extension.

---

# Very low-effort option: prompt template

A prompt template is the fastest thing to adopt right away.

## Proposed prompt template
File name:
- `cts-feature-audit.md`

Frontmatter:
```markdown
---
description: Add only non-duplicated, spec-conformant CTS tests for a language feature and mirror the final set into the release suite
argument-hint: "<feature> [frontend-test]"
---
```

Body:
```markdown
Task: Audit and add CTS tests for $1.

Hard requirements:
- Add only NEW, NON-DUPLICATED, SPEC-CONFORMANT tests
- Do not add characterization/current-behavior tests
- Do not add crash-only repros to CTS
- Deduplicate against the target folder and neighboring relevant chapters
- Name files by spec feature/sub-feature
- Mirror to ets-cts-7.0 only after final dedup
- If $2 is provided, port it only if the resulting CTS test is spec-conformant and non-duplicated

Process:
1. Locate implementation and spec clauses
2. Inventory existing tests
3. Produce delta matrix:
   spec clause | implementation locus | closest existing test | add/reuse/skip | reason
4. Add only delta tests in ets-templates
5. Mirror final set into ets-cts-7.0
6. Separately list any non-conformance/crash repros not added to CTS

Feature: $1
Frontend seed: $2
```

This would have prevented most of the back-and-forth in this session.

---

# My recommendation

## Immediate
Use the prompt template above.

## Best long-term
Build a pi extension:
- **name:** `cts-delta-auditor`
- **purpose:** spec-first CTS delta generation with semantic dedup and release-suite mirroring

## Why this is the best payoff
It directly targets the three things that cost the most time here:
1. distinguishing CTS-safe vs repro-only tests
2. duplicate / near-duplicate detection
3. final-only mirroring into release suite

If you want, I can next draft the actual **SKILL.md** or the actual **pi extension design** for `cts-delta-auditor`.

content.text:
Yes — for **finding implementation inconsistencies**, the framing matters **a lot**. More than for ordinary “add some tests” work.

In fact, I’d say:

> For exploratory compiler/runtime work, **the initial framing often matters more than the tool choice**.

## Why it matters

Because there are at least 3 very different search modes:

### 1. Coverage mode
Question is:
- “What spec-conformant tests are missing?”

This tends to produce:
- clean CTS additions
- conservative cases
- low-risk, low-novelty results

### 2. Falsification mode
Question is:
- “Where do spec and implementation disagree?”

This tends to produce:
- edge cases
- semantic boundary probes
- weird nested contexts
- good bug finds

### 3. Robustness mode
Question is:
- “What can crash/assert/miscompile?”

This tends to produce:
- adversarial inputs
- implementation-shaped repros
- non-CTS artifacts

Those modes overlap, but they are **not the same workflow**.  
If the agent starts in the wrong mode, it will explore the wrong space.

---

# In this session specifically

Your initial framing **did help**.

It pushed the work toward:
- reading spec + frontend implementation
- looking for semantic gaps
- checking nested RHS contexts
- checking generic constraint behavior
- checking arity and recursion edge cases

That is exactly the right direction for inconsistency hunting.

The problem was **not** the initial framing.

The real issue was that later the required deliverable became:

> “only final, non-duplicated, spec-conformant CTS tests”

So we had a **discovery-mode opening** but a **CTS-only finish**.

That mismatch caused extra loops:
- some interesting cases were found
- but then later rejected as non-CTS / too implementation-shaped / too duplicative

So the lesson is:

> The framing absolutely matters — but the **artifact policy** must be separated from the **bug-hunting policy**.

---

# Best framework for finding inconsistencies

For this kind of work, I’d use a **two-phase framework**:

## Phase A — inconsistency discovery
Goal:
- find spec/impl mismatches, diagnostic inconsistencies, crash paths

Output:
- a findings matrix
- minimized repro snippets
- no CTS edits yet

## Phase B — harvesting
Goal:
- convert only the valid subset into CTS tests

Output:
- spec-conformant, non-duplicate tests only

This separation is the key.

---

# A good inconsistency-finding framework

I think the best mental model is:

## Spec → Invariant → Variant → Oracle → Classification

### 1. Spec
Extract the actual semantic claims.

For type aliases, examples are:
- alias names introduce type declarations
- generic alias instantiation must respect arity
- generic constraints must be enforced through aliases
- circular alias references are invalid
- recursion checks should not depend on irrelevant syntactic wrapping

### 2. Invariant
Turn each spec claim into a testable invariant.

Examples:
- `C<int>` and `type A = C<int>` must enforce the same constraint rule
- circularity should be rejected whether recursion appears:
  - directly
  - in tuple element
  - in union member
  - in function position
  - in type argument position
- alias use should preserve generic arity checks

### 3. Variant
Generate semantically related variants around the same rule.

For example, for recursion:
- direct self-reference
- tuple-contained self-reference
- union-contained self-reference
- function-type-contained self-reference
- nested generic-contained self-reference

This is where many inconsistencies show up.

### 4. Oracle
Define what counts as “same” or “wrong”.

There are several possible oracles:

- **spec oracle**: pass/fail per spec
- **equivalence oracle**: semantically equivalent forms should behave the same
- **diagnostic oracle**: same rule should produce same or compatible diagnostic
- **robustness oracle**: no crash/assert

### 5. Classification
Bucket results into:
- spec-conformant behavior
- spec violation
- diagnostic inconsistency
- crash/assert
- duplicate/known-covered
- unclear / spec-ambiguous

That makes downstream decisions much easier.

---

# The most effective way to “frame direction of thinking”

If your real goal is to find inconsistencies, the prompt should explicitly put the agent in **falsification mode**.

For example:

> Work in spec-first falsification mode.  
> Do not optimize for adding CTS immediately.  
> First derive invariants from spec, then generate semantically equivalent or boundary variants, then compare implementation behavior.  
> Classify findings into:
> - spec violation
> - diagnostic inconsistency
> - crash/assert
> - already covered
> Only after that, identify which findings can be turned into spec-conformant CTS tests.

That framing changes the search behavior a lot.

Without it, an agent often defaults to:
- nearby-file imitation
- small local edits
- existing test pattern extension

That is good for coverage work, but weaker for inconsistency discovery.

---

# A very important distinction: “find bugs” vs “write CTS”

If you mix them in one undifferentiated prompt, the agent will constantly trade off between:
- “interesting counterexample”
- “portable spec test”
- “minimal repro”
- “non-duplicated suite addition”

That causes churn.

Much better is:

## Mode 1: Hunt
- be adversarial
- generate weird semantic variants
- follow suspicious implementation branches
- minimize failures

## Mode 2: Harvest
- discard non-spec artifacts
- deduplicate
- normalize naming
- mirror into CTS

That is the clean split.

---

# For inconsistency-finding, framing matters even more than for normal coding

For normal coding:
- the target file/function usually constrains the task a lot already

For inconsistency-finding:
- the search space is huge
- the “interestingness” criteria are ambiguous
- the agent can easily spend time on low-value corners

So yes, direction-of-thinking matters **a lot**.

I’d summarize it like this:

## If you frame it as:
### “Add tests for feature X”
You get:
- safe coverage
- fewer surprises
- fewer bug finds

### “Find spec/impl inconsistencies for feature X”
You get:
- more semantic exploration
- more edge cases
- more bug candidates
- but also more non-CTS artifacts

### “Find inconsistencies, then harvest CTS-safe deltas only”
You get the best combined workflow.

---

# Best practical framework for you

I’d recommend using this exact structure next time:

## Stage 1 — discovery
Ask for:
- relevant spec clauses
- relevant implementation loci
- semantic invariants
- boundary/context matrix
- candidate counterexamples
- classification of outcomes

## Stage 2 — CTS harvest
Ask for:
- only spec-conformant, non-duplicate tests
- no characterization tests
- no crash-only repros
- mirror only final set

---

# Example of a strong initial prompt

```text
Goal: find implementation inconsistencies for feature X.

Work in spec-first falsification mode.

Phase A:
1. Read the relevant spec clauses and implementation loci.
2. Derive semantic invariants and equivalence relations.
3. Generate a boundary/context matrix.
4. Probe the implementation with minimal examples.
5. Classify each finding as:
   - spec violation
   - diagnostic inconsistency
   - crash/assert
   - already covered
   - unclear/spec-ambiguous

Do not edit CTS yet.

Phase B:
From the findings, add only the NEW, NON-DUPLICATED, SPEC-CONFORMANT CTS tests.
Do not add characterization tests or crash-only repros to CTS.
Mirror to ets-cts-7.0 only after the final deduplicated set is ready.
```

That would steer the work much better.

---

# If you want a reusable framework in pi

I’d actually separate this into **two reusable helpers**:

## 1. `spec-gap-hunter`
Purpose:
- inconsistency discovery

Would help with:
- spec clause extraction
- invariant generation
- semantic variant generation
- result clustering
- crash/spec/diagnostic classification

## 2. `cts-delta-auditor`
Purpose:
- harvest only CTS-safe coverage deltas

Would help with:
- dedup audit
- naming normalization
- template/release mirroring

That split maps exactly to the two modes above.

---

# Short answer

So: **yes, it matters a lot**.

Your first prompt’s “direction of thinking” was useful because it put the work into **inconsistency-discovery mode**.  
What would make it even better is to explicitly separate:

- **discover inconsistencies**
from
- **convert only valid findings into CTS**

That separation is the real efficiency gain.

If you want, I can next draft a concrete **“spec-gap-hunter” skill/extension design** specifically for compiler/frontend inconsistency hunting.

And in another similar session LLM straightfully found and added into CTS tests that crash current implementation. Was extra-high level of thinking of LLM was a reason of such different behavior?

one more question before skill/extension design: at some moment LLM thought I want only current-implementation passing tests, but I never stated that (I actually needed the opposite). And in another similar session LLM straightfully found and added into CTS tests that crash current implementation. Was extra-high level of thinking of LLM was a reason of such different behavior?

**Explaining model behavior**

I need to address the user's query about why the model's tests and crash tests differ in the CTS. This isn't just about "extra-high-level thinking" but more related to ambiguous latent policies and search heuristics. There could also be factors like underspecified tasks, conflicting objectives, and optimization pressures. I should clarify the test suite norms and provide guidance on prevention. Additionally, mentioning how "LLM planning style" may lead to over-interpretation could be helpful in understanding the inconsistencies.

**Analyzing test assumptions**

When asked to add tests, the model might think they should pass based on the expectation that contributions make the codebase "green." This leads to differences in behavior, influenced by hidden defaults, local cues in the prompt, examples from the code repository, and earlier discussions. There's also artifact ambiguity—some tests are conformance-based, while others expect failures or compiler errors. I should clarify that the model's reasoning isn't necessarily "extra-high-level"; it infers latent intent from ambiguous constraints, which can amplify wrong assumptions if the premises aren't clear.

**Clarifying reasoning and objectives**

I understand that better reasoning can lead to increased consistency when an objective is fixed, but it may confidently optimize for the wrong inferred objective when that's not the case. So, "higher-level thinking" can indeed amplify incorrect assumptions. The core issue stems from underspecification and mismatches in default priorities. For example, asking to "add tests to CTS" typically implies green conformance tests, but negative compiler tests are acceptable. When instructing to "find bugs," the model might add crashers, thinking they're valuable, although crashing is usually not acceptable.

**Exploring defaults and behavior**

I’m thinking about the engineering defaults for preserving green builds and the research/fuzzer default to surface the strongest failures. The user's prompt wasn't clear on which default governs CTS writes. Should I mention “temperature” or stochasticity? The environment's deterministic nature seems relevant. Small context differences matter, and even the same model can behave differently over time due to conversation history. 

When it comes to high-level reasoning, it might not be the reason for different behavior; rather, hidden objectives and context likely play a role.

**Clarifying test conditions**

When an agent encounters a CTS, it might infer a conformance suite. On the other hand, when it sees negative tests with `_n` and identifies inconsistencies, it may view it as an adversarial bug hunt. There’s a need for nuance because CTS can include negative tests that fail to compile but may still adhere to specs. Crash tests should not be included. 

I suggest a protocol to establish clear conditions upfront, requiring user confirmation before proceeding. This could be structured into a concise answer covering key points.

**Addressing CTS clarity**

I need to make sure I respond to the user's need by directly addressing their comment about “I actually needed the opposite” and the addition of another session into CTS tests that might crash the current implementation. The term "CTS" can be overloaded: for some, it represents a conformance suite that should remain aligned with the spec, while for others, it’s a way to document known regressions or negative behaviors. 

The agent might not know the project's policy unless it's clearly stated, especially with the presence of many `_n` negative tests that suggest failing tests are acceptable, without clarifying if crashes are permissible. Local examples, like files named `*_regression_n`, can also shape expectations.

**Exploring ambiguity in LLMs**

When an LLM faces ambiguity, it can choose from several implicit oracles: the current implementation, specifications, suite greenness, or bug exposure. Depending on the session or context, different oracles may be selected. This can lead to issues, especially when higher-level reasoning builds a plan around a wrong oracle instead of clarifying.

For instance, in one session, the current implementation and suite greenness might pass tests, while another session relying on specifications could lead to crashes. It's crucial to mention that "negative tests can pass" without confusion. So, I should prompt: "Use spec as the only oracle for the CTS."

**Rethinking test requirements**

I really need to clarify that the user didn't ask for current-implementation-passing tests, which means my earlier assumption was off. It seems I might have let some default software engineering habits influence my thinking. I should probably note that adding crash tests without explicit permission was also a misstep. I want to validate my understanding with the user, so let's craft my response with a moderate level of detail to ensure clarity.

content.text:
Yes — but the key issue is **not “extra-high-level thinking” by itself**.

The real issue is:

> the LLM had to choose an **implicit oracle** for what counts as a “good” test.

And under ambiguity, different sessions can choose different oracles.

---

# Short answer

You’re right:  
if you never said “I want tests that pass current implementation”, then inferring that was a mistake.

And if another session added **crashing tests directly into CTS**, that was also usually a mistake unless you explicitly allowed crash repros in CTS.

So the difference was **not mainly intelligence level**.  
It was more:

- ambiguous task framing
- different session context/history
- different default assumptions about what a test suite is for

---

# The core concept: which oracle is the model optimizing for?

For this kind of task, there are several possible hidden “oracles”:

## 1. Implementation oracle
“Good tests are those that match current implementation behavior.”

This leads to:
- characterization tests
- current-behavior tests
- “what passes now?” mindset

## 2. Spec oracle
“Good tests are those required by the language spec, even if implementation fails them.”

This leads to:
- conformance tests
- spec-first negatives
- tests that may expose bugs

## 3. CI/green-suite oracle
“Good tests are those that can be committed without breaking the tree.”

This leads to:
- conservative additions
- avoidance of known failing cases
- tendency to align with current behavior unless explicitly told otherwise

## 4. Bug-hunting oracle
“Good tests are those that expose the strongest failure: mismatch, assert, crash.”

This leads to:
- adversarial inputs
- crash repros
- sometimes putting too much value on “interesting failures”

---

# What likely happened in your two sessions

## Session A: it assumed current-implementation-passing tests
That usually happens when the model implicitly combines:

- “I’m editing a repo”
- “I’m adding tests”
- “tests should be committable”
- “don’t break the suite”

So it silently drifts toward:

> “probably they want tests that pass now, or negative tests already supported by current implementation”

That is a **common coding-assistant default**.

It’s wrong for spec-first conformance work unless explicitly confirmed.

---

## Session B: it added crashers into CTS
That usually happens when the model implicitly combines:

- “find inconsistencies”
- “find bugs”
- “crash is a strong bug signal”
- “negative tests exist in this suite already”

Then it drifts toward:

> “the most valuable test is the one that demonstrates the failure most clearly”

That is a **common bug-hunting default**.

Again: useful for discovery, but wrong for CTS unless the suite policy explicitly allows such tests.

---

# So was “higher-level thinking” a factor?

## Yes, but only indirectly
If by “higher-level thinking” you mean:

- making abstract inferences from incomplete cues
- deciding intent without asking
- building a coherent plan around that inferred intent

then yes, that can amplify the problem.

But the deeper problem is not “too much intelligence”.

It is:

> **unverified objective inference**

A more reasoning-heavy model can actually be **more confidently wrong** if it infers the wrong objective and then optimizes hard for it.

So I’d phrase it this way:

- **Better reasoning helps once the objective is fixed**
- **Better reasoning hurts if the objective is guessed incorrectly**

That is probably what you observed.

---

# Why the same user can get opposite behaviors in similar sessions

Even small context differences can flip the model’s default.

Examples:

## If the prompt emphasizes:
- “add tests”
- “extend coverage”
- “prepare commit-ready changes”

the model tends to favor:
- green-suite / current-impl-compatible behavior

## If the prompt emphasizes:
- “find inconsistencies”
- “find crashes”
- “look for missing checks”
- “follow implementation paths”

the model tends to favor:
- adversarial / bug-hunting behavior

## If the repo contains:
- many `_n` negative tests
- regression-like names
- compiler diagnostic checks

the model may generalize:
- “failing compile is acceptable”
and then overstep into:
- “crash repro is also acceptable”

even though those are very different.

---

# Important nuance: “failing test” is overloaded

This matters a lot.

There are at least 4 different things people mean:

## 1. Passing positive CTS test
Program is valid and compiles/runs as expected.

## 2. Passing negative CTS test
Program is invalid and the compiler emits the expected diagnostic.

This is still a **green** CTS test.

## 3. Spec-conformant test that current implementation fails
This is a **valid conformance test candidate**, but may not be commit-ready if the suite must stay green.

## 4. Crash/assert repro
This is usually **not** a CTS test.
It is a bug repro.

LLMs often blur #2, #3, and #4 unless the policy is stated explicitly.

---

# So what should have been explicit?

For this kind of work, the most important thing is to lock the oracle.

A good instruction is:

> For CTS, use the **spec as the only oracle**.  
> Current implementation behavior is **not** the oracle.  
> Negative CTS tests are allowed if they expect a spec-required diagnostic.  
> Crash/assert repros must be reported separately and must not be added to CTS.

That one paragraph eliminates most of the confusion.

---

# Best way to prevent this in future sessions

Before any edits, force the agent to restate the policy.

For example:

```text
Before editing, state which oracle you are using for candidate tests:
- spec oracle
- current implementation oracle
- green-suite oracle
- bug-hunting oracle

For this task:
- CTS must use the spec oracle
- current implementation behavior must not be used as acceptance criteria
- crash/assert repros are out of CTS and should be reported separately
- if a candidate is spec-valid but currently fails implementation, classify it separately before adding anything
```

That would make this kind of drift much less likely.

---

# My honest diagnosis of the earlier misread

I think the model likely made this chain of assumptions:

1. “They want tests added to repository”
2. “Repository edits should usually preserve suite health”
3. “So I should prefer tests compatible with current behavior”
4. “Unless explicitly told otherwise”

That is a standard coding-agent prior.

It was not supported by what you actually wanted.

So yes: that inference was avoidable and should have been checked rather than assumed.

---

# The best single sentence to include next time

If you want to prevent both failure modes, add this sentence early:

> Use the **spec**, not the current implementation, as the oracle for CTS; do **not** add crash/assert repros to CTS, and report them separately.

That one line disambiguates almost everything.

---

# Condensed answer

So:

- **No**, the difference was not mainly because one model “thought at a higher level.”
- **Yes**, higher-level inference can amplify the problem if the model guesses the wrong goal.
- The real cause was **implicit oracle selection under ambiguity**:
  - one session chose “green/current behavior”
  - another chose “strongest bug signal”
- For this work, the most important thing is to explicitly fix:
  - **spec oracle for CTS**
  - **separate bucket for crash repros**
  - **separate bucket for current non-conformance findings**

If you want, the next thing I can do is design the skill/extension around exactly this idea:  
**first-class oracle selection + candidate classification before any test generation.**
yes, design skill/extension

yes, design skill/extension

content.text:
pi.dev domain graciously donated by


exe.dev

> New issues and PRs from new contributors are auto-closed by default. Maintainers review auto-closed issues daily. See [CONTRIBUTING.md](../../CONTRIBUTING.md). --- Pi is a minimal terminal coding harness. Adapt pi to your workflows, not the other way around, without having to fork and modify pi internals. Extend it with TypeScript [Extensions](#extensions), [Skills](#skills), [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates), and [Themes](#themes). Put your extensions, skills, prompt templates, and themes in [Pi Packages](#pi-packages) and share them with others via npm or git. Pi ships with powerful defaults but skips features like sub agents and plan mode. Instead, you can ask pi to build what you want or install a third party pi package that matches your workflow. Pi runs in four modes: interactive, print or JSON, RPC for process integration, and an SDK for embedding in your own apps. See [openclaw/openclaw](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw) for a real-world SDK integration. ## Share your OSS coding agent sessions If you use pi for open source work, please share your coding agent sessions. Public OSS session data helps improve models, prompts, tools, and evaluations using real development workflows. For the full explanation, see [this post on X](https://x.com/badlogicgames/status/2037811643774652911). To publish sessions, use [`badlogic/pi-share-hf`](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-share-hf). Read its README.md for setup instructions. All you need is a Hugging Face account, the Hugging Face CLI, and `pi-share-hf`. You can also watch [this video](https://x.com/badlogicgames/status/2041151967695634619), where I show how I publish my `pi-mono` sessions. I regularly publish my own `pi-mono` work sessions here: - [badlogicgames/pi-mono on Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/datasets/badlogicgames/pi-mono) ## Table of Contents - [Quick Start](#quick-start) - [Providers & Models](#providers--models) - [Interactive Mode](#interactive-mode) - [Editor](#editor) - [Commands](#commands) - [Keyboard Shortcuts](#keyboard-shortcuts) - [Message Queue](#message-queue) - [Sessions](#sessions) - [Branching](#branching) - [Compaction](#compaction) - [Settings](#settings) - [Context Files](#context-files) - [Customization](#customization) - [Prompt Templates](#prompt-templates) - [Skills](#skills) - [Extensions](#extensions) - [Themes](#themes) - [Pi Packages](#pi-packages) - [Programmatic Usage](#programmatic-usage) - [Philosophy](#philosophy) - [CLI Reference](#cli-reference) --- ## Quick Start ```bash npm install -g @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent ``` Authenticate with an API key: ```bash export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-... pi ``` Or use your existing subscription: ```bash pi /login # Then select provider ``` Then just talk to pi. By default, pi gives the model four tools: `read`, `write`, `edit`, and `bash`. The model uses these to fulfill your requests. Add capabilities via [skills](#skills), [prompt templates](#prompt-templates), [extensions](#extensions), or [pi packages](#pi-packages). **Platform notes:** [Windows](docs/windows.md) | [Termux (Android)](docs/termux.md) | [tmux](docs/tmux.md) | [Terminal setup](docs/terminal-setup.md) | [Shell aliases](docs/shell-aliases.md) --- ## Providers & Models For each built-in provider, pi maintains a list of tool-capable models, updated with every release. Authenticate via subscription (`/login`) or API key, then select any model from that provider via `/model` (or Ctrl+L). **Subscriptions:** - Anthropic Claude Pro/Max - OpenAI ChatGPT Plus/Pro (Codex) - GitHub Copilot - Google Gemini CLI - Google Antigravity **API keys:** - Anthropic - OpenAI - Azure OpenAI - Google Gemini - Google Vertex - Amazon Bedrock - Mistral - Groq - Cerebras - xAI - OpenRouter - Vercel AI Gateway - ZAI - OpenCode Zen - OpenCode Go - Hugging Face - Kimi For Coding - MiniMax See [docs/providers.md](docs/providers.md) for detailed setup instructions. **Custom providers & models:** Add providers via `~/.pi/agent/models.json` if they speak a supported API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google). For custom APIs or OAuth, use extensions. See [docs/models.md](docs/models.md) and [docs/custom-provider.md](docs/custom-provider.md). --- ## Interactive Mode

The interface from top to bottom: - **Startup header** - Shows shortcuts (`/hotkeys` for all), loaded AGENTS.md files, prompt templates, skills, and extensions - **Messages** - Your messages, assistant responses, tool calls and results, notifications, errors, and extension UI - **Editor** - Where you type; border color indicates thinking level - **Footer** - Working directory, session name, total token/cache usage, cost, context usage, current model The editor can be temporarily replaced by other UI, like built-in `/settings` or custom UI from extensions (e.g., a Q&A tool that lets the user answer model questions in a structured format). [Extensions](#extensions) can also replace the editor, add widgets above/below it, a status line, custom footer, or overlays. ### Editor | Feature | How | |---------|-----| | File reference | Type `@` to fuzzy-search project files | | Path completion | Tab to complete paths | | Multi-line | Shift+Enter (or Ctrl+Enter on Windows Terminal) | | Images | Ctrl+V to paste (Alt+V on Windows), or drag onto terminal | | Bash commands | `!command` runs and sends output to LLM, `!!command` runs without sending | Standard editing keybindings for delete word, undo, etc. See [docs/keybindings.md](docs/keybindings.md). ### Commands Type `/` in the editor to trigger commands. [Extensions](#extensions) can register custom commands, [skills](#skills) are available as `/skill:name`, and [prompt templates](#prompt-templates) expand via `/templatename`. | Command | Description | |---------|-------------| | `/login`, `/logout` | OAuth authentication | | `/model` | Switch models | | `/scoped-models` | Enable/disable models for Ctrl+P cycling | | `/settings` | Thinking level, theme, message delivery, transport | | `/resume` | Pick from previous sessions | | `/new` | Start a new session | | `/name ` | Set session display name | | `/session` | Show session info (file, ID, messages, tokens, cost) | | `/tree` | Jump to any point in the session and continue from there | | `/fork` | Create a new session from a previous user message | | `/clone` | Duplicate the current active branch into a new session | | `/compact [prompt]` | Manually compact context, optional custom instructions | | `/copy` | Copy last assistant message to clipboard | | `/export [file]` | Export session to HTML file | | `/share` | Upload as private GitHub gist with shareable HTML link | | `/reload` | Reload keybindings, extensions, skills, prompts, and context files (themes hot-reload automatically) | | `/hotkeys` | Show all keyboard shortcuts | | `/changelog` | Display version history | | `/quit` | Quit pi | ### Keyboard Shortcuts See `/hotkeys` for the full list. Customize via `~/.pi/agent/keybindings.json`. See [docs/keybindings.md](docs/keybindings.md). **Commonly used:** | Key | Action | |-----|--------| | Ctrl+C | Clear editor | | Ctrl+C twice | Quit | | Escape | Cancel/abort | | Escape twice | Open `/tree` | | Ctrl+L | Open model selector | | Ctrl+P / Shift+Ctrl+P | Cycle scoped models forward/backward | | Shift+Tab | Cycle thinking level | | Ctrl+O | Collapse/expand tool output | | Ctrl+T | Collapse/expand thinking blocks | ### Message Queue Submit messages while the agent is working: - **Enter** queues a *steering* message, delivered after the current assistant turn finishes executing its tool calls - **Alt+Enter** queues a *follow-up* message, delivered only after the agent finishes all work - **Escape** aborts and restores queued messages to editor - **Alt+Up** retrieves queued messages back to editor On Windows Terminal, `Alt+Enter` is fullscreen by default. Remap it in [docs/terminal-setup.md](docs/terminal-setup.md) so pi can receive the follow-up shortcut. Configure delivery in [settings](docs/settings.md): `steeringMode` and `followUpMode` can be `"one-at-a-time"` (default, waits for response) or `"all"` (delivers all queued at once). `transport` selects provider transport preference (`"sse"`, `"websocket"`, or `"auto"`) for providers that support multiple transports. --- ## Sessions Sessions are stored as JSONL files with a tree structure. Each entry has an `id` and `parentId`, enabling in-place branching without creating new files. See [docs/session.md](docs/session.md) for file format. ### Management Sessions auto-save to `~/.pi/agent/sessions/` organized by working directory. ```bash pi -c # Continue most recent session pi -r # Browse and select from past sessions pi --no-session # Ephemeral mode (don't save) pi --session # Use specific session file or ID pi --fork # Fork specific session file or ID into a new session ``` Use `/session` in interactive mode to see the current session ID before reusing it with `--session ` or `--fork `. ### Branching **`/tree`** - Navigate the session tree in-place. Select any previous point, continue from there, and switch between branches. All history preserved in a single file.

- Search by typing, fold/unfold and jump between branches with Ctrl+←/Ctrl+→ or Alt+←/Alt+→, page with ←/→ - Filter modes (Ctrl+O): default → no-tools → user-only → labeled-only → all - Press Shift+L to label entries as bookmarks and Shift+T to toggle label timestamps **`/fork`** - Create a new session file from a previous user message on the active branch. Opens a selector, copies the active path up to that point, and places the selected prompt in the editor for modification. **`/clone`** - Duplicate the current active branch into a new session file at the current position. The new session keeps the full active-path history and opens with an empty editor. **`--fork `** - Fork an existing session file or partial session UUID directly from the CLI. This copies the full source session into a new session file in the current project. ### Compaction Long sessions can exhaust context windows. Compaction summarizes older messages while keeping recent ones. **Manual:** `/compact` or `/compact ` **Automatic:** Enabled by default. Triggers on context overflow (recovers and retries) or when approaching the limit (proactive). Configure via `/settings` or `settings.json`. Compaction is lossy. The full history remains in the JSONL file; use `/tree` to revisit. Customize compaction behavior via [extensions](#extensions). See [docs/compaction.md](docs/compaction.md) for internals. --- [364 more lines in file. Use offset=261 to continue.]

> pi can create skills. Ask it to build one for your use case.

# Skills

Skills are self-contained capability packages that the agent loads on-demand. A skill provides specialized workflows, setup instructions, helper scripts, and reference documentation for specific tasks.

Pi implements the [Agent Skills standard](https://agentskills.io/specification), warning about violations but remaining lenient.

## Table of Contents

- [Locations](#locations)
- [How Skills Work](#how-skills-work)
- [Skill Commands](#skill-commands)
- [Skill Structure](#skill-structure)
- [Frontmatter](#frontmatter)
- [Validation](#validation)
- [Example](#example)
- [Skill Repositories](#skill-repositories)

## Locations

> **Security:** Skills can instruct the model to perform any action and may include executable code the model invokes. Review skill content before use.

Pi loads skills from:

- Global:
  - `~/.pi/agent/skills/`
  - `~/.agents/skills/`
- Project:
  - `.pi/skills/`
  - `.agents/skills/` in `cwd` and ancestor directories (up to git repo root, or filesystem root when not in a repo)
- Packages: `skills/` directories or `pi.skills` entries in `package.json`
- Settings: `skills` array with files or directories
- CLI: `--skill <path>` (repeatable, additive even with `--no-skills`)

Discovery rules:
- In `~/.pi/agent/skills/` and `.pi/skills/`, direct root `.md` files are discovered as individual skills
- In all skill locations, directories containing `SKILL.md` are discovered recursively
- In `~/.agents/skills/` and project `.agents/skills/`, root `.md` files are ignored

Disable discovery with `--no-skills` (explicit `--skill` paths still load).

### Using Skills from Other Harnesses

To use skills from Claude Code or OpenAI Codex, add their directories to settings:

```json
{
  "skills": [
    "~/.claude/skills",
    "~/.codex/skills"
  ]
}
```

For project-level Claude Code skills, add to `.pi/settings.json`:

```json
{
  "skills": ["../.claude/skills"]
}
```

## How Skills Work

1. At startup, pi scans skill locations and extracts names and descriptions
2. The system prompt includes available skills in XML format per the [specification](https://agentskills.io/integrate-skills)
3. When a task matches, the agent uses `read` to load the full SKILL.md (models don't always do this; use prompting or `/skill:name` to force it)
4. The agent follows the instructions, using relative paths to reference scripts and assets

This is progressive disclosure: only descriptions are always in context, full instructions load on-demand.

## Skill Commands

Skills register as `/skill:name` commands:

```bash
/skill:brave-search           # Load and execute the skill
/skill:pdf-tools extract      # Load skill with arguments
```

Arguments after the command are appended to the skill content as `User: <args>`.

Toggle skill commands via `/settings` in interactive mode or in `settings.json`:

```json
{
  "enableSkillCommands": true
}
```

## Skill Structure

A skill is a directory with a `SKILL.md` file. Everything else is freeform.

```
my-skill/
├── SKILL.md              # Required: frontmatter + instructions
├── scripts/              # Helper scripts
│   └── process.sh
├── references/           # Detailed docs loaded on-demand
│   └── api-reference.md
└── assets/
    └── template.json
```

### SKILL.md Format

````markdown
---
name: my-skill
description: What this skill does and when to use it. Be specific.
---

# My Skill

## Setup

Run once before first use:
```bash
cd /path/to/skill && npm install
```

## Usage

```bash
./scripts/process.sh <input>
```
````

Use relative paths from the skill directory:

```markdown
See [the reference guide](references/REFERENCE.md) for details.
```

## Frontmatter

Per the [Agent Skills specification](https://agentskills.io/specification#frontmatter-required):

| Field | Required | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | Yes | Max 64 chars. Lowercase a-z, 0-9, hyphens. Must match parent directory. |
| `description` | Yes | Max 1024 chars. What the skill does and when to use it. |
| `license` | No | License name or reference to bundled file. |
| `compatibility` | No | Max 500 chars. Environment requirements. |
| `metadata` | No | Arbitrary key-value mapping. |
| `allowed-tools` | No | Space-delimited list of pre-approved tools (experimental). |
| `disable-model-invocation` | No | When `true`, skill is hidden from system prompt. Users must use `/skill:name`. |

### Name Rules

- 1-64 characters
- Lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens only
- No leading/trailing hyphens
- No consecutive hyphens
- Must match parent directory name

Valid: `pdf-processing`, `data-analysis`, `code-review`
Invalid: `PDF-Processing`, `-pdf`, `pdf--processing`

### Description Best Practices

The description determines when the agent loads the skill. Be specific.

Good:
```yaml
description: Extracts text and tables from PDF files, fills PDF forms, and merges multiple PDFs. Use when working with PDF documents.
```

Poor:
```yaml
description: Helps with PDFs.
```

## Validation

Pi validates skills against the Agent Skills standard. Most issues produce warnings but still load the skill:

- Name doesn't match parent directory
- Name exceeds 64 characters or contains invalid characters
- Name starts/ends with hyphen or has consecutive hyphens
- Description exceeds 1024 characters

Unknown frontmatter fields are ignored.

**Exception:** Skills with missing description are not loaded.

Name collisions (same name from different locations) warn and keep the first skill found.

## Example

```
brave-search/
├── SKILL.md
├── search.js
└── content.js
```

**SKILL.md:**
````markdown
---
name: brave-search
description: Web search and content extraction via Brave Search API. Use for searching documentation, facts, or any web content.
---

# Brave Search

## Setup

```bash
cd /path/to/brave-search && npm install
```

## Search

```bash
./search.js "query"              # Basic search
./search.js "query" --content    # Include page content
```

## Extract Page Content

```bash
./content.js https://example.com
```
````

## Skill Repositories

- [Anthropic Skills](https://github.com/anthropics/skills) - Document processing (docx, pdf, pptx, xlsx), web development
- [Pi Skills](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-skills) - Web search, browser automation, Google APIs, transcription

> pi can create prompt templates. Ask it to build one for your workflow.

# Prompt Templates

Prompt templates are Markdown snippets that expand into full prompts. Type `/name` in the editor to invoke a template, where `name` is the filename without `.md`.

## Locations

Pi loads prompt templates from:

- Global: `~/.pi/agent/prompts/*.md`
- Project: `.pi/prompts/*.md`
- Packages: `prompts/` directories or `pi.prompts` entries in `package.json`
- Settings: `prompts` array with files or directories
- CLI: `--prompt-template <path>` (repeatable)

Disable discovery with `--no-prompt-templates`.

## Format

```markdown
---
description: Review staged git changes
---
Review the staged changes (`git diff --cached`). Focus on:
- Bugs and logic errors
- Security issues
- Error handling gaps
```

- The filename becomes the command name. `review.md` becomes `/review`.
- `description` is optional. If missing, the first non-empty line is used.
- `argument-hint` is optional. When set, the hint is displayed before the description in the autocomplete dropdown.

### Argument Hints

Use `argument-hint` in frontmatter to show expected arguments in autocomplete. Use `<angle brackets>` for required arguments and `[square brackets]` for optional ones:

```markdown
---
description: Review PRs from URLs with structured issue and code analysis
argument-hint: "<PR-URL>"
---
```

This renders in the autocomplete dropdown as:

```
→ pr   <PR-URL>       — Review PRs from URLs with structured issue and code analysis
  is   <issue>        — Analyze GitHub issues (bugs or feature requests)
  wr   [instructions] — Finish the current task end-to-end
  cl   — Audit changelog entries before release
```

## Usage

Type `/` followed by the template name in the editor. Autocomplete shows available templates with descriptions.

```
/review                           # Expands review.md
/component Button                 # Expands with argument
/component Button "click handler" # Multiple arguments
```

## Arguments

Templates support positional arguments and simple slicing:

- `$1`, `$2`, ... positional args
- `$@` or `$ARGUMENTS` for all args joined
- `${@:N}` for args from the Nth position (1-indexed)
- `${@:N:L}` for `L` args starting at N

Example:

```markdown
---
description: Create a component
---
Create a React component named $1 with features: $@
```

Usage: `/component Button "onClick handler" "disabled support"`

## Loading Rules

- Template discovery in `prompts/` is non-recursive.
- If you want templates in subdirectories, add them explicitly via `prompts` settings or a package manifest.

**Exploring tool extensions**

I need to take a closer look at examples or extensions related to tools and commands as well as session states. It's probably a good idea to inspect the names of these extensions. content.text:
# Extension Examples

Example extensions for pi-coding-agent.

## Usage

```bash
# Load an extension with --extension flag
pi --extension examples/extensions/permission-gate.ts

# Or copy to extensions directory for auto-discovery
cp permission-gate.ts ~/.pi/agent/extensions/
```

## Examples

### Lifecycle & Safety

| Extension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `permission-gate.ts` | Prompts for confirmation before dangerous bash commands (rm -rf, sudo, etc.) |
| `protected-paths.ts` | Blocks writes to protected paths (.env, .git/, node_modules/) |
| `confirm-destructive.ts` | Confirms before destructive session actions (clear, switch, fork) |
| `dirty-repo-guard.ts` | Prevents session changes with uncommitted git changes |
| `sandbox/` | OS-level sandboxing using `@anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime` with per-project config |

### Custom Tools

| Extension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `todo.ts` | Todo list tool + `/todos` command with custom rendering and state persistence |
| `hello.ts` | Minimal custom tool example |
| `question.ts` | Demonstrates `ctx.ui.select()` for asking the user questions with custom UI |
| `questionnaire.ts` | Multi-question input with tab bar navigation between questions |
| `tool-override.ts` | Override built-in tools (e.g., add logging/access control to `read`) |
| `dynamic-tools.ts` | Register tools after startup (`session_start`) and at runtime via command, with prompt snippets and tool-specific prompt guidelines |
| `built-in-tool-renderer.ts` | Custom compact rendering for built-in tools (read, bash, edit, write) while keeping original behavior |
| `minimal-mode.ts` | Override built-in tool rendering for minimal display (only tool calls, no output in collapsed mode) |
| `truncated-tool.ts` | Wraps ripgrep with proper output truncation (50KB/2000 lines) |
| `antigravity-image-gen.ts` | Generate images via Google Antigravity with optional save-to-disk modes |
| `ssh.ts` | Delegate all tools to a remote machine via SSH using pluggable operations |
| `subagent/` | Delegate tasks to specialized subagents with isolated context windows |

### Commands & UI

| Extension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `preset.ts` | Named presets for model, thinking level, tools, and instructions via `--preset` flag and `/preset` command |
| `plan-mode/` | Claude Code-style plan mode for read-only exploration with `/plan` command and step tracking |
| `tools.ts` | Interactive `/tools` command to enable/disable tools with session persistence |
| `handoff.ts` | Transfer context to a new focused session via `/handoff <goal>` |
| `qna.ts` | Extracts questions from last response into editor via `ctx.ui.setEditorText()` |
| `status-line.ts` | Shows turn progress in footer via `ctx.ui.setStatus()` with themed colors |
| `widget-placement.ts` | Shows widgets above and below the editor via `ctx.ui.setWidget()` placement |
| `hidden-thinking-label.ts` | Customizes the collapsed thinking label via `ctx.ui.setHiddenThinkingLabel()` |
| `working-indicator.ts` | Customizes the streaming working indicator via `ctx.ui.setWorkingIndicator()` |
| `model-status.ts` | Shows model changes in status bar via `model_select` hook |
| `snake.ts` | Snake game with custom UI, keyboard handling, and session persistence |
| `tic-tac-toe.ts` | Tic-tac-toe vs the agent with `executionMode: "sequential"` tools to prevent race conditions on shared cursor state |
| `send-user-message.ts` | Demonstrates `pi.sendUserMessage()` for sending user messages from extensions |
| `timed-confirm.ts` | Demonstrates AbortSignal for auto-dismissing `ctx.ui.confirm()` and `ctx.ui.select()` dialogs |
| `rpc-demo.ts` | Exercises all RPC-supported extension UI methods; pair with [`examples/rpc-extension-ui.ts`](../rpc-extension-ui.ts) |
| `modal-editor.ts` | Custom vim-like modal editor via `ctx.ui.setEditorComponent()` |
| `rainbow-editor.ts` | Animated rainbow text effect via custom editor |
| `notify.ts` | Desktop notifications via OSC 777 when agent finishes (Ghostty, iTerm2, WezTerm) |
| `titlebar-spinner.ts` | Braille spinner animation in terminal title while the agent is working |
| `summarize.ts` | Summarize conversation with GPT-5.2 and show in transient UI |
| `custom-footer.ts` | Custom footer with git branch and token stats via `ctx.ui.setFooter()` |
| `custom-header.ts` | Custom header via `ctx.ui.setHeader()` |
| `overlay-test.ts` | Test overlay compositing with inline text inputs and edge cases |
| `overlay-qa-tests.ts` | Comprehensive overlay QA tests: anchors, margins, stacking, overflow, animation |
| `doom-overlay/` | DOOM game running as an overlay at 35 FPS (demonstrates real-time game rendering) |
| `shutdown-command.ts` | Adds `/quit` command demonstrating `ctx.shutdown()` |
| `reload-runtime.ts` | Adds `/reload-runtime` and `reload_runtime` tool showing safe reload flow |
| `interactive-shell.ts` | Run interactive commands (vim, htop) with full terminal via `user_bash` hook |
| `inline-bash.ts` | Expands `!{command}` patterns in prompts via `input` event transformation |

### Git Integration

| Extension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `git-checkpoint.ts` | Creates git stash checkpoints at each turn for code restoration on fork |
| `auto-commit-on-exit.ts` | Auto-commits on exit using last assistant message for commit message |

### System Prompt & Compaction

| Extension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `pirate.ts` | Demonstrates `systemPromptAppend` to dynamically modify system prompt |
| `claude-rules.ts` | Scans `.claude/rules/` folder and lists rules in system prompt |
| `custom-compaction.ts` | Custom compaction that summarizes entire conversation |
| `trigger-compact.ts` | Triggers compaction when context usage exceeds 100k tokens and adds `/trigger-compact` command |

### System Integration

| Extension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `mac-system-theme.ts` | Syncs pi theme with macOS dark/light mode |

### Resources

| Extension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `dynamic-resources/` | Loads skills, prompts, and themes using `resources_discover` |

### Messages & Communication

| Extension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `message-renderer.ts` | Custom message rendering with colors and expandable details via `registerMessageRenderer` |
| `event-bus.ts` | Inter-extension communication via `pi.events` |

### Session Metadata

| Extension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `session-name.ts` | Name sessions for the session selector via `setSessionName` |
| `bookmark.ts` | Bookmark entries with labels for `/tree` navigation via `setLabel` |

### Custom Providers

| Extension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `custom-provider-anthropic/` | Custom Anthropic provider with OAuth support and custom streaming implementation |
| `custom-provider-gitlab-duo/` | GitLab Duo provider using pi-ai's built-in Anthropic/OpenAI streaming via proxy |
| `custom-provider-qwen-cli/` | Qwen CLI provider with OAuth device flow and OpenAI-compatible models |

### External Dependencies

| Extension | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `with-deps/` | Extension with its own package.json and dependencies (demonstrates jiti module resolution) |
| `file-trigger.ts` | Watches a trigger file and injects contents into conversation |

## Writing Extensions

See [docs/extensions.md](../../docs/extensions.md) for full documentation.

```typescript
import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent";
import { Type } from "@sinclair/typebox";

export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {
  // Subscribe to lifecycle events
  pi.on("tool_call", async (event, ctx) => {
    if (event.toolName === "bash" && event.input.command?.includes("rm -rf")) {
      const ok = await ctx.ui.confirm("Dangerous!", "Allow rm -rf?");
      if (!ok) return { block: true, reason: "Blocked by user" };
    }
  });

  // Register custom tools
  pi.registerTool({
    name: "greet",
    label: "Greeting",
    description: "Generate a greeting",
    parameters: Type.Object({
      name: Type.String({ description: "Name to greet" }),
    }),
    async execute(toolCallId, params, onUpdate, ctx, signal) {
      return {
        content: [{ type: "text", text: `Hello, ${params.name}!` }],
        details: {},
      };
    },
  });

  // Register commands
  pi.registerCommand("hello", {
    description: "Say hello",
    handler: async (args, ctx) => {
      ctx.ui.notify("Hello!", "info");
    },
  });
}
```

## Key Patterns

**Use StringEnum for string parameters** (required for Google API compatibility):
```typescript
import { StringEnum } from "@mariozechner/pi-ai";

// Good
action: StringEnum(["list", "add"] as const)

// Bad - doesn't work with Google
action: Type.Union([Type.Literal("list"), Type.Literal("add")])
```

**State persistence via details:**
```typescript
// Store state in tool result details for proper forking support
return {
  content: [{ type: "text", text: "Done" }],
  details: { todos: [...todos], nextId },  // Persisted in session
};

// Reconstruct on session events
pi.on("session_start", async (_event, ctx) => {
  for (const entry of ctx.sessionManager.getBranch()) {
    if (entry.type === "message" && entry.message.toolName === "my_tool") {
      const details = entry.message.details;
      // Reconstruct state from details
    }
  }
});
```

# Plan Mode Extension

Read-only exploration mode for safe code analysis.

## Features

- **Read-only tools**: Restricts available tools to read, bash, grep, find, ls, question
- **Bash allowlist**: Only read-only bash commands are allowed
- **Plan extraction**: Extracts numbered steps from `Plan:` sections
- **Progress tracking**: Widget shows completion status during execution
- **[DONE:n] markers**: Explicit step completion tracking
- **Session persistence**: State survives session resume

## Commands

- `/plan` - Toggle plan mode
- `/todos` - Show current plan progress
- `Ctrl+Alt+P` - Toggle plan mode (shortcut)

## Usage

1. Enable plan mode with `/plan` or `--plan` flag
2. Ask the agent to analyze code and create a plan
3. The agent should output a numbered plan under a `Plan:` header:

```
Plan:
1. First step description
2. Second step description
3. Third step description
```

4. Choose "Execute the plan" when prompted
5. During execution, the agent marks steps complete with `[DONE:n]` tags
6. Progress widget shows completion status

## How It Works

### Plan Mode (Read-Only)
- Only read-only tools available
- Bash commands filtered through allowlist
- Agent creates a plan without making changes

### Execution Mode
- Full tool access restored
- Agent executes steps in order
- `[DONE:n]` markers track completion
- Widget shows progress

### Command Allowlist

Safe commands (allowed):
- File inspection: `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `less`, `more`
- Search: `grep`, `find`, `rg`, `fd`
- Directory: `ls`, `pwd`, `tree`
- Git read: `git status`, `git log`, `git diff`, `git branch`
- Package info: `npm list`, `npm outdated`, `yarn info`
- System info: `uname`, `whoami`, `date`, `uptime`

Blocked commands:
- File modification: `rm`, `mv`, `cp`, `mkdir`, `touch`
- Git write: `git add`, `git commit`, `git push`
- Package install: `npm install`, `yarn add`, `pip install`
- System: `sudo`, `kill`, `reboot`
- Editors: `vim`, `nano`, `code`
