content.text:
I’d build this as a **small pi package with both an extension and two skills**.

Why hybrid:

- **Skill** = tells the model *how to think*
- **Extension** = prevents the model from silently drifting to the wrong oracle/policy
- **Prompt templates** = make startup fast and consistent

For your use case, **extension-only** is not enough, because the hard part is not just automation — it is getting the model into the right **spec-first falsification mode**.  
And **skill-only** is not enough, because it won’t enforce guardrails like “don’t put crash repros into CTS”.

---

# Recommended package

## Name
`pi-conformance-lab`

## Contents
```text
pi-conformance-lab/
├── package.json
├── extensions/
│   └── conformance-lab/
│       └── index.ts
├── skills/
│   ├── spec-gap-hunter/
│   │   ├── SKILL.md
│   │   └── references/
│   │       ├── oracles.md
│   │       ├── classifications.md
│   │       └── invariant-matrix.md
│   └── cts-delta-harvest/
│       ├── SKILL.md
│       └── references/
│           ├── dedup.md
│           ├── naming.md
│           └── mirroring.md
└── prompts/
    ├── gap.md
    └── harvest.md
```

## `package.json`
```json
{
  "name": "pi-conformance-lab",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "pi": {
    "extensions": ["./extensions/conformance-lab/index.ts"],
    "skills": ["./skills"],
    "prompts": ["./prompts"]
  }
}
```

---

# Core idea: explicit oracle policy

This is the center of the whole design.

The extension should make the agent choose a policy **before** it starts generating or editing tests.

## Policy object
```ts
type OraclePolicy = {
  discoveryMode: "spec-first-falsification" | "impl-first" | "robustness";
  ctsOracle: "spec-only" | "current-implementation";
  suiteGate: "green-only" | "allow-known-failing-spec";
  crashPolicy: "report-only" | "separate-repro";
  mirrorPolicy: "final-only";
  namingPolicy: "spec-feature-subfeature";
  dedupRoots: string[];
};
```

## Default preset for your workflow
```ts
{
  discoveryMode: "spec-first-falsification",
  ctsOracle: "spec-only",
  suiteGate: "green-only",
  crashPolicy: "report-only",
  mirrorPolicy: "final-only",
  namingPolicy: "spec-feature-subfeature",
  dedupRoots: [
    "target feature folder",
    "neighboring spec chapter folders",
    "related generics/ownership folders"
  ]
}
```

This prevents the exact failure mode you hit:
- model silently assuming “current implementation is the oracle”
- model silently assuming “strongest bug signal belongs in CTS”

---

# Responsibility split

## 1. Extension: enforce policy and automate batch work
The extension should do four things:

### A. Pin the oracle
When `/gap-start` is invoked, it asks the user a few structured questions via `ctx.ui` and stores the answers in session state.

### B. Provide batch tools
Instead of many small `read`/`bash` calls, it gives the model tools that do:
- spec + impl + tests inventory
- candidate probing
- dedup clustering
- mirroring

### C. Persist findings in session state
Use tool result `details` so state survives:
- resume
- session branching
- forks

### D. Guard CTS writes
Use a `tool_call` hook to block or warn on:
- editing `ets-cts-*` before approval
- writing crash repros into CTS paths
- file names containing `impl`, `difference`, `regression`, `crash` in CTS areas

That is the concrete mechanism that turns your intent into something enforceable.

---

# Extension commands

I’d register these custom commands:

## `/gap-start <feature> [seed]`
Starts a guided setup:
- feature name
- optional frontend seed test
- discovery mode
- CTS oracle
- suite gate
- dedup roots
- mirror target roots

At the end it writes a concise session policy summary.

---

## `/gap-policy`
Shows current policy and lets you edit it.

Useful because policy drift is the real source of confusion.

---

## `/gap-findings`
Shows a compact UI board:

```text
Feature: type aliases
Discovery: spec-first-falsification
CTS oracle: spec-only
Suite gate: green-only

Findings
- 3 spec-negative CTS candidates
- 1 spec-positive CTS candidate
- 2 duplicates
- 1 crash repro
- 1 ambiguous/spec-unclear
```

---

## `/gap-approve <id...>`
Marks findings as CTS-harvestable.

This is important: **mirroring and CTS writes should only use approved findings**.

---

## `/gap-mirror`
Copies the final approved set from `ets-templates` to `ets-cts-*`.

Blocked unless:
- findings exist
- candidates are approved
- dedup status is clean
- mirror policy allows it

---

## `/gap-export`
Exports the findings matrix as Markdown or JSON.

Good for bug reports and review notes.

---

# Extension custom tools

I’d keep the tool set small and batch-oriented.

## 1. `gap_scan_surface`
Purpose: one-shot inventory.

### Input
- feature
- spec roots
- implementation roots
- test roots
- optional seed files

### Output
```json
{
  "specClauses": [...],
  "implementationLoci": [...],
  "existingTests": [...],
  "relatedTests": [...],
  "suggestedAxes": [...]
}
```

### Example axes
- direct vs nested recursion
- generic arity exact vs too many vs too few
- constraint on class vs interface vs primitive
- alias RHS contexts: tuple / union / function / type arg / array

This reduces the first huge exploration phase.

---

## 2. `gap_probe_cases`
Purpose: run compiler/front-end probes in batch.

### Input
- compiler command
- working directory
- list of snippets/cases
- timeout

### Output
```json
{
  "results": [
    {
      "name": "tuple-recursion",
      "status": "semantic_error",
      "diagCode": "ESE0311",
      "message": "Circular type alias reference"
    },
    {
      "name": "bad-constraint",
      "status": "semantic_error",
      "diagCode": "ESE0228",
      "message": "Type argument 'Int' should be a subtype..."
    },
    {
      "name": "weird-nested-case",
      "status": "crash"
    }
  ]
}
```

This lets the model probe a whole matrix with one tool call.

---

## 3. `gap_dedup`
Purpose: semantic-nearest-neighbor check against existing tests.

### Input
- candidate snippets or approved findings
- existing test inventory
- dedup roots

### Output
```json
{
  "matches": [
    {
      "candidateId": "F-7",
      "nearest": [
        {
          "path": ".../spec_n2.ets",
          "score": 0.89,
          "reason": "same negative rule, same recursion context"
        }
      ],
      "decisionHint": "likely-duplicate"
    }
  ]
}
```

For MVP, the fingerprint can be heuristic:
- positive/negative
- feature
- subfeature
- recursion context
- arity condition
- constraint kind
- expected diagnostic code

It does not need to be perfect to be useful.

---

## 4. `gap_record_finding`
Purpose: persist a finding with classification.

### Input
- invariant
- snippet
- probe result
- closest tests
- classification

### Output
- finding ID
- persisted summary

### Classification enum
- `spec-positive-cts`
- `spec-negative-cts`
- `spec-violation`
- `diagnostic-inconsistency`
- `crash-repro`
- `duplicate`
- `ambiguous`

This is the other critical piece: **classification before generation**.

---

## 5. `gap_harvest_cts`
Purpose: turn approved findings into real template files.

### Input
- finding IDs
- target dir
- naming policy
- parametrize or one-file mode

### Output
- proposed file set
- preview or written paths

This tool should refuse:
- `crash-repro`
- `diagnostic-inconsistency`
- `spec-violation` when `suiteGate=green-only`

unless the policy explicitly allows otherwise.

---

## 6. `gap_sync_release`
Purpose: final mirroring step only.

### Input
- source files
- destination root

### Output
- synced file list

---

# Guardrails

These are what make the extension worth building.

## Guardrail 1: CTS path protection
If a write/edit targets:
- `ets-cts-*`
and there are no approved CTS-eligible findings, block it.

---

## Guardrail 2: crash repro protection
If a candidate is classified as:
- `crash-repro`
and the model tries to write it into CTS, block it.

Suggest:
- store as repro note
- open separate issue artifact
- keep out of CTS

---

## Guardrail 3: policy banner
Show a small persistent widget or status line like:

```text
Mode: spec-first-falsification | CTS oracle: spec-only | crash->report-only | mirror: final-only
```

This keeps the model anchored.

---

## Guardrail 4: naming policy
Warn on names like:
- `*_impl_*`
- `*_difference_*`
- `*_crash_*`
- `*_regression_*`

inside CTS target paths.

---

# Skill 1: `spec-gap-hunter`

This skill teaches the reasoning process.

## Frontmatter
```markdown
---
name: spec-gap-hunter
description: Find spec/implementation inconsistencies for a language feature using spec-first falsification. Use before writing CTS tests.
---
```

## Main instructions
The skill should tell the model to:

1. Read nearest `AGENTS.md`
2. Read relevant spec clauses
3. Locate frontend/runtime implementation loci
4. Derive semantic invariants
5. Build a boundary/context matrix
6. Probe cases in batches
7. Record findings with explicit classification
8. Do **not** edit CTS yet

## Key rule in the skill
```markdown
Never use current implementation behavior as the CTS oracle unless the active policy explicitly says so.
Crash/assert findings are not CTS tests unless the active policy explicitly allows that.
```

That line is essential.

---

# Skill 2: `cts-delta-harvest`

This skill handles the second phase only.

## Frontmatter
```markdown
---
name: cts-delta-harvest
description: Convert approved findings into new, non-duplicated, spec-conformant CTS tests and mirror the final set to release suite.
---
```

## Main instructions
1. Load approved findings only
2. Run dedup against declared roots
3. Skip exact and near duplicates
4. Generate only CTS-eligible files
5. Name by feature/subfeature
6. Mirror only after final review

This cleanly separates **hunt** from **harvest**.

---

# Prompt templates

## `gap.md`
```markdown
---
description: Start spec-first inconsistency discovery for a language feature
argument-hint: "<feature> [seed-file]"
---
Use the conformance workflow for feature: $1
Seed file: $2

First:
- initialize policy for spec-first falsification
- do not edit CTS yet
- scan spec, implementation, and existing tests
- produce invariant/context matrix
- classify findings before proposing edits
```

## `harvest.md`
```markdown
---
description: Harvest approved findings into spec-conformant CTS deltas
argument-hint: "<feature>"
---
For feature $1:
- use approved findings only
- generate only non-duplicated, spec-conformant CTS tests
- keep crash repros and non-conformance reports out of CTS
- mirror to release suite only after final set is complete
```

---

# Example workflow

## Discovery
```text
/gap type-alias /home/.../32651.ets
/skill:spec-gap-hunter
```

The extension:
- asks policy questions
- pins the oracle
- gives the model scan/probe/dedup tools

The model:
- derives invariants
- probes cases
- records findings

---

## Harvest
```text
/gap-findings
/gap-approve F-3 F-4 F-8
/skill:cts-delta-harvest
/gap-mirror
```

This is exactly the split that would have made your earlier session much cleaner.

---

# What to build first

## MVP
Build only:
- extension policy state
- `/gap-start`
- `/gap-findings`
- `gap_scan_surface`
- `gap_probe_cases`
- `gap_record_finding`
- CTS path guard

That already fixes most of the ambiguity problem.

## V2
Add:
- `gap_dedup`
- `gap_harvest_cts`
- `gap_sync_release`

## V3
Add:
- richer semantic fingerprints
- issue/repro export
- automatic nearest-test suggestions

---

# My recommendation
If you want one concrete deliverable, build:

- **Extension:** `conformance-lab`
- **Skills:** `spec-gap-hunter`, `cts-delta-harvest`
- **Prompt templates:** `gap`, `harvest`

That gives you:
- correct framing
- explicit oracle selection
- reproducible workflow
- guardrails against the two failure modes you saw

If you want, next I can draft the actual:
1. `SKILL.md` files, or  
2. `extensions/conformance-lab/index.ts` skeleton.

Let's simplify design: I need only to find inconsistencies in implementation. And crash on spec-conformant meaningful test instead of compile-error report or successfull compilation is one of inconsistencies.

content.text:
Agreed. With that scope, the design gets much smaller.

And yes:

> **Crash/assert/hang on a meaningful spec-grounded test with a well-defined expected outcome is a first-class inconsistency.**

So we no longer need CTS harvesting, dedup-for-release, mirroring, or “green suite” policy.

---

# Simplified target

## Best fit now
**Build one skill first, plus an optional lightweight extension.**

- **Skill** = teaches the model the right search method
- **Extension** = batches probing and tracks findings cleanly

If you want the highest ROI with lowest complexity:
1. build **`spec-gap-hunter` skill**
2. optionally add a tiny **`spec-gap` extension**

---

# Core rule set

## Oracle
- **The spec is the oracle**
- current implementation behavior is **evidence**, not oracle

## What counts as an inconsistency
A finding is an inconsistency if a meaningful, spec-grounded test produces any of these:

1. **rejects-valid**
   - spec says program is valid
   - implementation reports compile error

2. **accepts-invalid**
   - spec says program is invalid
   - implementation accepts it

3. **wrong-diagnostic**
   - spec says reject
   - implementation rejects, but with clearly wrong phase/category/diagnostic

4. **equivalent-forms-diverge**
   - semantically equivalent or same-rule contexts behave differently

5. **internal-failure**
   - crash / assert / ICE / hang
   - on a meaningful spec-grounded test with a defined expected outcome

6. **ambiguous-spec**
   - implementation looks suspicious, but spec is not

content.text:
Then yes — the design gets much simpler.

If the goal is **only to find implementation inconsistencies**, I would **drop the CTS/harvest half completely** and keep just a **spec-first gap-hunting workflow**.

And yes, in this simplified model:

> **Crash/assert/hang on a meaningful spec-grounded test is an inconsistency**  
> whenever the spec implies a normal outcome:
> - successful compilation/execution, or
> - ordinary parse/semantic error reporting

So the tool should treat crashes as a **first-class result category**, not as a side case.

---

# Simplified recommendation

## Best fit now
Build:

1. **one skill**: `spec-gap-hunter`
2. optionally **one small extension**: `inconsistency-lab`
3. optionally **one prompt template**: `gap`

You no longer need:
- CTS gating
- mirroring
- dedup-to-release logic
- naming policy enforcement

---

# What the workflow should optimize for

The skill/extension should optimize for this question:

> “Given a spec clause or derived invariant, where does the implementation behave inconsistently?”

Not:
- “what tests can I commit?”
- “what passes today?”
- “what is nearest to existing tests?”

That means the **oracle must be the spec**, not the implementation.

---

# What counts as an inconsistency

I’d make this explicit in the design.

For each candidate case, the skill should record:

## Expected outcome from spec
One of:
- `accept`
- `reject`
- `unclear`

## Observed implementation outcome
One of:
- `accept`
- `reject`
- `wrong-diagnostic`
- `crash`
- `assert`
- `hang`
- `inconsistent-with-equivalent-case`

## Inconsistency rule
A finding is an inconsistency if:

- spec says `accept`, impl rejects/crashes/asserts/hangs
- spec says `reject`, impl accepts/crashes/asserts/hangs
- spec says `reject`, impl rejects but with clearly wrong phase/rule/diagnostic
- semantically equivalent or spec-equivalent forms behave differently
- same rule behaves differently across nested contexts without spec basis

That last one is very important for compiler work.

---

# Meaningful test policy

You used an important word: **meaningful**.

The skill should define that as:

> A meaningful inconsistency probe is grounded in a spec clause, a derived invariant, or a semantic equivalence relation.

So it should **prefer**:
- variant generation around a real rule
- nested/contextual transformations
- alias/direct equivalence
- boundary arity/constraint/recursion forms

And **avoid**:
- random malformed garbage
- unsupported foreign syntax unless explicitly testing parser robustness
- pure crash fuzz without spec anchor

That keeps the search high-value.

---

# Revised design

## 1. Skill: `spec-gap-hunter`

This is now the main artifact.

### Purpose
Teach the model to:
- read spec + implementation
- derive invariants
- generate meaningful variants
- probe behavior
- classify inconsistencies

### Frontmatter
```markdown
---
name: spec-gap-hunter
description: Find spec/implementation inconsistencies for a language feature using spec-first falsification. Use for compiler/frontend/runtime conformance investigations, including crashes on meaningful spec-grounded tests.
---
```

### Core instructions
I’d put something like this in `SKILL.md`:

````markdown
# Spec Gap Hunter

## Objective

Use the specification as the only oracle.

Current implementation behavior is evidence, not ground truth.

Your job is to find inconsistencies between:
- the spec
- semantic invariants derived from the spec
- the observed implementation behavior

A crash/assert/hang on a meaningful spec-grounded test is an inconsistency when the spec implies a normal non-crashing outcome.

## What counts as meaningful

Prefer candidates that are grounded in:
- an explicit spec clause
- a semantic invariant
- an equivalence relation
- a boundary condition of a specified rule

Avoid random malformed syntax unless the task explicitly asks for robustness/fuzzing.

## Workflow

1. Read the nearest AGENTS.md files.
2. Read the relevant spec clauses fully.
3. Locate implementation loci for the feature.
4. Derive semantic invariants.
5. Build a boundary/context matrix.
6. Generate small meaningful variants.
7. Probe the implementation.
8. For each result, record:
   - spec clause
   - invariant
   - candidate code
   - expected outcome
   - observed outcome
   - classification
   - likely implementation locus
9. Minimize the repro while preserving the same inconsistency.
10. Report findings in a compact matrix.

## Allowed outcome classes

Expected:
- accept
- reject
- unclear

Observed:
- accept
- reject
- wrong-diagnostic
- crash
- assert
- hang
- inconsistent-with-equivalent-case

## Classification

Use one of:
- spec-violation-accepts-invalid
- spec-violation-rejects-valid
- wrong-diagnostic
- crash-on-valid
- crash-on-invalid
- assert-on-valid
- assert-on-invalid
- hang
- context-sensitive-inconsistency
- ambiguous-spec

## Preferred search strategy

Start from a real rule, then vary one semantic axis at a time.

Typical axes:
- direct vs aliased form
- direct vs nested context
- class vs interface vs primitive constraint
- exact vs missing vs extra type arguments
- top-level vs tuple vs union vs function vs generic-argument position
- direct reference vs semantically equivalent wrapped reference

Do not immediately write tests into CTS or product test suites unless explicitly asked.
````

That alone would already improve consistency a lot.

---

# 2. Optional small extension: `inconsistency-lab`

If you want actual productivity gains, add a **small extension**, but much smaller than the earlier CTS-aware design.

## Purpose
The extension should only do three things:

### A. Pin the oracle
Make the active policy visible:
- spec-first
- meaningful probes only
- crashes count as inconsistencies

### B. Batch compiler probing
Give the model a tool to compile many candidate snippets in one call.

### C. Persist findings
Store findings in session state so they survive resume/fork.

That’s enough.

---

# Minimal extension design

## Commands

### `/gap-start <feature> [seed]`
Sets session mode:
- feature name
- optional seed file
- compiler command
- spec roots
- source roots

And writes a status line like:
```text
Gap mode: spec-first | meaningful probes only | crashes count as inconsistencies
```

---

### `/gap-findings`
Shows recorded findings.

---

### `/gap-export`
Exports findings as Markdown or JSON.

---

## Tools

### `probe_cases`
This is the most valuable tool.

#### Input
- compiler command
- temp dir or working dir
- list of cases:
  - name
  - code
  - optional filename
  - optional expected category

#### Output
```json
{
  "results": [
    {
      "name": "alias-through-constraint",
      "status": "reject",
      "diagCode": "ESE0228",
      "message": "Type argument 'Int' should be a subtype..."
    },
    {
      "name": "tuple-recursive-alias",
      "status": "crash"
    }
  ]
}
```

This replaces lots of repetitive `write temp file -> run compiler -> inspect output`.

---

### `record_finding`
Stores:

```json
{
  "feature": "type aliases",
  "specClause": "4_names ...",
  "invariant": "constraint enforcement must be preserved through aliasing",
  "candidateName": "alias-class-constraint-int",
  "expected": "reject",
  "observed": "reject",
  "classification": "none"
}
```

or

```json
{
  "expected": "reject",
  "observed": "crash",
  "classification": "crash-on-invalid"
}
```

or

```json
{
  "expected": "accept",
  "observed": "crash",
  "classification": "crash-on-valid"
}
```

---

### `scan_surface`
Optional, but useful.

It gathers:
- relevant spec files
- relevant source files
- nearby tests
- candidate search axes

This helps the model start from structure instead of wandering.

---

# Why this extension is enough

Because for inconsistency-hunting, the expensive parts are:

- keeping the oracle straight
- probing many cases quickly
- not losing track of findings

You do **not** need the heavier CTS machinery.

---

# Suggested prompt template

A small prompt template is still useful.

## `gap.md`
```markdown
---
description: Find spec/implementation inconsistencies for a language feature
argument-hint: "<feature> [seed-file]"
---
Investigate inconsistencies for feature: $1
Seed file: $2

Use spec-first falsification mode.
Treat the spec as the only oracle.
Treat crash/assert/hang on a meaningful spec-grounded case as an inconsistency.
Do not optimize for current implementation behavior.
Do not write CTS tests unless explicitly asked.

Process:
1. Read relevant spec clauses and implementation loci
2. Derive semantic invariants
3. Build a boundary/context matrix
4. Probe meaningful variants
5. Classify findings
6. Minimize the strongest repros
7. Summarize inconsistencies in a compact table
```

---

# Concrete result format

The skill should force output into a stable table like this:

| ID | Spec clause | Invariant | Variant axis | Expected | Observed | Classification | Likely locus |
|----|-------------|-----------|--------------|----------|----------|----------------|--------------|
| F1 | 4_names/... | generic constraints preserved through aliasing | direct vs aliased | reject | reject | none | checker/... |
| F2 | 15_semantics/... | circular alias rejection independent of RHS wrapping | direct vs tuple element | reject | crash | crash-on-invalid | etsTypeAliasType |
| F3 | 5_generics/... | arity preserved through alias use | direct vs alias instantiation | reject | accept | spec-violation-accepts-invalid | typeCheckingHelpers |

That makes sessions much easier to steer.

---

# Search strategy the skill should teach

This is probably the most important part.

The skill should explicitly tell the model:

## Don’t generate random tests first
Instead:

### 1. derive an invariant
Example:
- “Generic class constraints must still be enforced when referenced through a type alias.”

### 2. pick one semantic axis
Example:
- direct use vs type alias
- top-level vs tuple vs union vs function context

### 3. vary one thing at a time
This is how you get useful inconsistencies instead of noisy chaos.

### 4. keep the smallest reproducer
That matters a lot for bug reports.

---

# Best simplified package structure

```text
pi-inconsistency-lab/
├── package.json
├── extensions/
│   └── inconsistency-lab/
│       └── index.ts
├── skills/
│   └── spec-gap-hunter/
│       ├── SKILL.md
│       └── references/
│           ├── invariants.md
│           ├── classifications.md
│           └── probing.md
└── prompts/
    └── gap.md
```

---

# My recommendation

For your simplified goal:

## Build first
- `spec-gap-hunter` skill
- `gap.md` prompt template

## Add second, if needed
- tiny `inconsistency-lab` extension with:
  - `/gap-start`
  - `/gap-findings`
  - `probe_cases`
  - `record_finding`

That is the smallest design that still meaningfully improves this workflow.

---

# Bottom line

With your clarified goal, the right design is:

> **spec-first inconsistency hunter**, not conformance-test harvester

and the central rule is:

> **crash/assert/hang on a meaningful spec-grounded case is itself a finding**

If you want, next I can draft either:

1. the actual `skills/spec-gap-hunter/SKILL.md`, or  
2. the minimal `extensions/inconsistency-lab/index.ts` skeleton.

**Defining eligibility criteria**

I need to clearly define what it means to be "eligible for ETS templates." This involves ensuring there's a strong specification clause or derived invariant, that cases are not already covered or nearly covered, that they can be expressed in the suite harness, and that I can provide meaningful minimal examples. 

I realize that if an implementation crashes on a valid or invalid case, it may still be considered eligible. But I must note a caveat about crashes or hangs: if adding such tests destabilizes the harness or impacts reporting, it might be better to track these as separate issues until the harness can isolate the problems effectively.

**Designing a compliance structure**

I think it's helpful to mention "language compliance" subtly without overdoing it. I plan to answer with a design that includes two separate predicates: "suite-eligibility" and "current-impl-status." Next, I'll craft a minimal design with one skill and an optional integrated workflow. A "decision table" could outline actions based on specifications. Lastly, I'd propose revised tools and commands, without needing to mirror anything to ets-cts.

**Refining response structure**

I need to consider if the user might want to add a gate for ets-cts later. I think it’s crucial to respond directly to their requirement without asking too many questions. **Clarifying test handling**

I think if the suite consists of only source inputs with expected compile/runtime results, then a crash indicates that there's a bug, and I should include it. However, if a crash or hang stops batch runs, I might need to handle that differently and mention the caveat lightly. I could also reference "non-duplicated with existing" for near-duplicate semantics. It might help to define deduplication based on rules or invariants, rather than just the text. That seems clearer!

I'm outlining the skill `ets-spec-gap-hunter`, focusing on its purpose and detailed rules. There's a suite eligibility that's met if it's spec-grounded, unique, meaningful, and expressible. content.text:
Got it. That changes the policy in one very important way:

> **`ets-templates` should be treated as a spec-compliance suite, not a “must-pass-on-current-HEAD” suite.**

So the acceptance rule is **not**:

- “does current implementation pass this?”

It is:

- **is it spec-based?**
- **is it meaningful?**
- **is it non-duplicated?**

If yes, it is a valid suite candidate **even if current implementation rejects it, misdiagnoses it, crashes, or hangs**.

---

# The right model now

You need two separate dimensions:

## 1. Suite eligibility
This decides whether a case belongs in `ets-templates`.

A case is suite-eligible if it is:

1. **spec-based**
   - directly grounded in a spec clause, or
   - grounded in a solid invariant derived from the spec

2. **meaningful**
   - not random malformed garbage
   - not unsupported foreign syntax unless the spec/harness actually intends to cover that
   - minimal and semantically relevant

3. **non-duplicated**
   - not already covered by an existing test or near-equivalent test

## 2. Current implementation status
This is just metadata about the finding:

- passes
- fails with expected error
- fails with wrong error
- wrongly accepts
- crashes
- asserts
- hangs

This dimension should **not** decide suite eligibility.

That is the core correction.

---

# So yes: crash can still mean “add this to ets-templates”

If the test is:

- spec-based
- meaningful
- unique

and current implementation **crashes** on it instead of:
- accepting it, or
- emitting the expected ordinary diagnostic,

then that is a **strong compliance test candidate**.

In your workflow, a crash is not a reason to exclude the test.  
It is evidence of non-compliance.

The only caveat is practical:

- if the harness literally cannot run/report such a test without breaking the whole run machinery, then you may need a temporary repro-only bucket
- but that is a **harness limitation**, not a content limitation

Conceptually, the test still belongs in the compliance surface.

---

# Revised workflow

Now the workflow should be:

## Phase A — find inconsistencies
- read spec
- read implementation
- derive invariants
- generate meaningful variants
- probe behavior
- classify findings

## Phase B — harvest into `ets-templates`
From the findings, add only those that are:
- spec-based
- meaningful
- non-duplicated

Do **not** filter them out just because current implementation fails.

---

# The key rule the skill/extension must enforce

This should be stated very explicitly:

> **Current implementation behavior is evidence, not the oracle.**  
> For `ets-templates`, the oracle is the spec.  
> A candidate should be rejected only if it is:
> - non-spec,
> - duplicate/near-duplicate,
> - ambiguous in spec basis,
> - or too malformed/non-meaningful for a compliance suite.

That one rule would have prevented the earlier wrong turn.

---

# Updated decision table

| Spec-based | Non-duplicate | Current impl result | Action |
|---|---:|---|---|
| yes | yes | passes | add |
| yes | yes | expected compile error | add |
| yes | yes | wrong diagnostic | add |
| yes | yes | wrongly accepts invalid program | add |
| yes | yes | wrongly rejects valid program | add |
| yes | yes | crash/assert/hang | add, if the case is meaningful and suite-expressible |
| yes | no | anything | skip as duplicate |
| unclear | any | anything | hold / investigate |
| no | any | crash | report only, do not add |

That’s the correct policy for a conformance-style suite.

---

# Revised design: simpler than full CTS, but not discovery-only

You no longer need the earlier “green CTS” assumptions.  
But you **do** still need harvesting.

So I’d recommend:

## one skill
`spec-gap-hunter`

## one optional extension
`ets-gap-lab`

## one prompt template
`gap`

That’s enough.

---

# Skill design

## Skill name
`spec-gap-hunter`

## What it teaches
The skill should tell the model to:

1. use the **spec as oracle**
2. derive invariants
3. probe meaningful semantic variants
4. record inconsistencies
5. harvest suite-eligible findings into `ets-templates`
6. reject candidates only for:
   - lack of spec basis
   - duplication
   - ambiguity
   - non-meaningfulness

## Crucial wording for `SKILL.md`
Something like:

```md
A candidate may be added to ets-templates even if current implementation fails, crashes, or hangs on it, provided the candidate is:
- spec-based,
- meaningful,
- and non-duplicated.

Do not use current implementation success as a gate for suite inclusion.
Use current implementation behavior only to classify the inconsistency.
```

That sentence is the heart of the workflow.

---

# Extension design

The extension is now much smaller than the original CTS-oriented one.

## Commands

### `/gap-start <feature> [seed-file]`
Sets session mode and shows:

- feature
- spec roots
- code roots
- test roots
- policy: `spec-only, add unique spec-based tests even if failing on current impl`

### `/gap-findings`
Shows findings table.

### `/gap-harvest`
Writes approved suite-eligible findings into `ets-templates`.

---

## Tools

### `scan_surface`
Batch inventory:
- spec clauses
- implementation loci
- existing tests
- nearby related tests
- candidate semantic axes

### `probe_cases`
Batch-run many candidate snippets and collect:
- accept
- reject
- diagnostic text/code
- crash/assert/hang

### `dedup_candidates`
Check candidates against existing `ets-templates` coverage semantically, not just textually.

### `write_template_tests`
Turn approved findings into actual `ets-templates` files.

---

# Most important extension rule

The extension should separate:

## `suiteEligible`
boolean based on:
- spec basis
- meaningfulness
- dedup status

from

## `currentOutcome`
one of:
- pass
- reject
- wrong-diagnostic
- wrong-accept
- wrong-reject
- crash
- assert
- hang

This separation is what prevents the model from drifting back into:
- “only passing tests”
or
- “only current behavior characterization”

---

# Dedup should be semantic, not textual

For your case, dedup should compare things like:

- same spec clause / same invariant
- same positive vs negative intent
- same primary semantic axis
- same nesting context
- same expected rule
- same diagnostic family, when relevant

Example:
- “generic class constraint through alias”
and
- “generic interface constraint through alias”

may be distinct enough.

But:
- two tuple-recursion negatives with nearly identical rule and context

may be duplicates even if text differs.

So the extension/skill should explicitly say:

> Treat near-equivalent coverage as duplicate unless the new case exercises a clearly different semantic axis.

---

# Minimal classification model

Each finding should carry:

- `specClause`
- `invariant`
- `candidate`
- `expectedBySpec`
- `observedInImpl`
- `duplicateStatus`
- `suiteEligible`
- `notes`

## Example
```json
{
  "specClause": "5_generics ...",
  "invariant": "constraint checking must be preserved through type alias use",
  "expectedBySpec": "reject",
  "observedInImpl": "crash",
  "duplicateStatus": "unique",
  "suiteEligible": true
}
```

That is exactly the kind of thing that should end up harvestable.

---

# Best prompt template

Use a prompt template that says this explicitly.

## `gap.md`
```md
---
description: Find spec-based implementation inconsistencies and harvest unique compliance tests
argument-hint: "<feature> [seed-file]"
---
Investigate feature: $1
Seed file: $2

Use the spec as the only oracle.

Goal:
1. Find meaningful spec-grounded inconsistencies in implementation
2. Harvest only those findings that are:
   - spec-based
   - non-duplicated
   - meaningful
   into ets-templates

Important:
- A test may be added to ets-templates even if current implementation fails, misdiagnoses, crashes, or hangs on it.
- Current implementation behavior is not the acceptance gate.
- Reject candidates only if they are non-spec, duplicate/near-duplicate, ambiguous, or not meaningful enough for a compliance suite.

Process:
1. Read spec clauses and implementation loci
2. Derive invariants
3. Build a boundary/context matrix
4. Probe meaningful variants
5. Classify findings
6. Deduplicate against existing ets-templates coverage
7. Harvest the unique suite-eligible findings into ets-templates
```

---

# Bottom line

Your clarified target is:

> **spec compliance suite population**, not “green tests only”

So the correct design principle is:

## Add if:
- spec-based
- meaningful
- non-duplicated

## Ignore current impl outcome as a gate:
- success
- ordinary error
- wrong diagnostic
- crash
- hang

That outcome is just **the finding**, not the filter.

If you want, next I can draft the actual `SKILL.md` for this revised workflow, or the minimal `ets-gap-lab` extension skeleton.

content.text:
No — **you do not really need an extension** for this.

For your current goal, I think **skill-first is the right design**.

## Why skill is enough
Your main problem is not missing UI/hooks/tooling. It is:

- choosing the **right oracle**: spec, not current impl
- following the **right workflow**: find gaps → dedup → harvest
- applying the **right filters**: spec-based, meaningful, non-duplicated
- not being misled by PR regression tests or current behavior

That is exactly what **skills** are good at: reusable workflow + instructions + helper scripts + reference docs.

An extension becomes useful only if you later want:
- enforced policy
- custom commands/UI
- persistent findings state
- batch custom tools beyond normal `bash/read/edit`

But you do **not** need that to start.

---

# My recommendation

Build a **small skill package**, not an extension.

## Package layout
```text
ets-compliance-skills/
├── skills/
│   ├── ets-spec-gap-hunter/
│   │   ├── SKILL.md
│   │   └── references/
│   │       ├── oracle.md
│   │       ├── dedup.md
│   │       ├── harvesting.md
│   │       └── finding-schema.md
│   └── ets-pr-backfill/
│       ├── SKILL.md
│       └── references/
│           ├── pr-triage.md
│           └── mapping-pr-tests-to-spec.md
├── shared/
│   ├── oracle.md
│   ├── dedup.md
│   ├── harvesting.md
│   ├── finding-schema.md
│   └── scripts/
│       ├── probe_cases.py
│       ├── find_related_tests.py
│       └── pr_surface.sh
└── prompts/
    ├── gap.md
    └── backfill-pr.md
```

You can even skip `prompts/` at first.

---

# Best design: 2 skills, shared core

## 1. Main skill: `ets-spec-gap-hunter`
Use this for normal work:
- start from a feature, not from a seed
- read spec + implementation
- derive invariants
- probe meaningful variants
- dedup against existing `ets-templates`
- harvest unique spec-based tests into `ets-templates`

### Input
- feature name
- optionally a path or area, but no seed required

### This should be the default skill.

---

## 2. Second skill: `ets-pr-backfill`
Use this when:
- a PR was merged
- it added/changed implementation tests
- but no `ets-templates` contribution was made
- and you want to backfill spec-based compliance coverage from that PR

### Important rule
This skill should treat PR tests as:

> **candidate sources**, not truth.

That means:
- do **not** blindly port PR tests into `ets-templates`
- first map them to spec clauses/invariants
- reject PR tests that are:
  - regression-only
  - impl-shaped
  - unsupported-syntax probes
  - duplicate of existing `ets-templates`

So yes, it should be a separate skill, but it should **reuse the same core rules**.

---

# Shared core rules both skills must reuse

These are the reusable parts you want to design now.

## Oracle
- spec is the oracle
- current implementation behavior is evidence only

## Harvest criteria
A test belongs in `ets-templates` if it is:
1. spec-based
2. meaningful
3. non-duplicated

## Current impl outcome is not a gate
Allowed harvest candidates may currently:
- pass
- reject
- misdiagnose
- crash
- assert
- hang

If they are spec-based and unique, they are still valid compliance tests.

## Dedup rule
Dedup must be semantic, not textual:
- same rule/invariant
- same positive/negative intent
- same context axis
- same effective coverage → duplicate

---

# Why 2 skills instead of 1
Because the starting point differs:

## Feature-driven mode
Start from:
- spec chapter
- code loci
- existing tests

## PR-backfill mode
Start from:
- merged PR diff / commit range / changed tests
- then map back to spec
- then dedup
- then harvest

Same core logic, different entry point.

So I would keep:
- one shared doctrine
- two separate skill entry points

That’s cleaner than one overloaded skill.

---

# What the shared files should contain

## `shared/oracle.md`
The most important file.

Should explicitly say:
- `ets-templates` is a language compliance suite
- spec is the oracle
- current implementation pass/fail is not the acceptance gate
- crash on meaningful spec-based input is a compliance failure, not an exclusion reason

---

## `shared/dedup.md`
How to compare candidates against existing coverage.

Criteria:
- same spec clause / invariant
- same axis
- same expected semantic rule
- same effective behavior class

With examples of:
- distinct enough
- near-duplicate
- duplicate

---

## `shared/harvesting.md`
How to turn findings into tests:
- prefer minimal repro
- prefer spec-feature/subfeature names
- skip impl-only names
- parameterize only when cases truly share one rule

---

## `shared/finding-schema.md`
Stable output format for both skills.

Example:
```text
specClause
invariant
candidate
expectedBySpec
observedNow
duplicateStatus
harvestDecision
notes
source: feature-scan | pr-backfill
```

This is what gives you reuse.

---

# Shared helper scripts
If you want reuse later, put reusable logic in **scripts**, not in the skill prose.

## `shared/scripts/probe_cases.py`
Batch-compile snippets with `es2panda` and report:
- accept
- reject
- diag
- crash/assert/hang

Useful for both skills.

## `shared/scripts/find_related_tests.py`
Given a feature/path, list likely duplicate surfaces:
- local chapter
- neighboring chapter
- generics/shared ownership dirs

Useful for both skills.

## `shared/scripts/pr_surface.sh`
Given a commit/range/merge commit:
- list changed files
- identify changed tests
- identify affected source/spec areas

Useful for `ets-pr-backfill`.

This is the best reuse boundary.

---

# Do you need prompt templates?
Not strictly.

Since pi skills can be invoked directly as:
- `/skill:ets-spec-gap-hunter type aliases`
- `/skill:ets-pr-backfill <rev-range>`

that may already be enough.

But prompt templates can be nice for convenience.

## Optional prompt template
### `gap.md`
```md
---
description: Find spec-based inconsistencies and harvest unique ets-templates tests
argument-hint: "<feature>"
---
Use the ets-spec-gap-hunter workflow for feature: $1
```

### `backfill-pr.md`
```md
---
description: Backfill ets-templates coverage from a merged PR
argument-hint: "<rev-range-or-merge-commit>"
---
Use the ets-pr-backfill workflow for: $1
```

---

# When an extension becomes worth it
Only later, if you repeatedly feel pain from:

1. too many repetitive probe tool calls  
2. too much manual dedup inventory  
3. wanting persistent findings across resume/fork  
4. wanting one custom command like `/backfill-pr`

If that happens, build a **tiny extension** that wraps the same shared scripts.

That’s important:  
if you ever add an extension later, it should be a **thin wrapper over the shared scripts/docs**, not a parallel redesign.

So the skill-first design still scales.

---

# Concrete recommendation

## Start with only this:
- `ets-spec-gap-hunter` skill
- `ets-pr-backfill` skill
- shared docs + scripts

## Do not build an extension yet.

That is the highest-value / lowest-complexity approach.

---

# Suggested skill roles

## `ets-spec-gap-hunter`
For feature-based work:
- identify spec clauses
- derive invariants
- probe variants
- dedup
- harvest to `ets-templates`

## `ets-pr-backfill`
For merged-PR backfill:
- inspect PR diff/tests
- extract candidate semantic scenarios
- map to spec/invariants
- reject impl-only cases
- dedup
- harvest to `ets-templates`

Both should explicitly reuse the same:
- oracle
- dedup rules
- harvesting rules
- finding schema

---

# My bottom-line answer

## Do you need an extension?
**No.**

## Is a skill enough?
**Yes, very likely.**  
For your problem, a skill is probably the **better first solution**.

## How to handle “backfill from previously merged PR”?
Use a **second skill** for that entry point, but make it reuse the same shared core materials and scripts as the main skill.

---

If you want, next I can draft:

1. `skills/ets-spec-gap-hunter/SKILL.md`  
2. `skills/ets-pr-backfill/SKILL.md`  

with shared rules tailored to `ets-templates`.
yes, implement these skills

# AGENTS.md

This file provides guidance to AI when working with code in this repository.

## Project Metadata

- **name**: Test Suite
- **purpose**: Testing infrastructure for runtime, compiler, irtoc, verifier, regression, and performance validation
- **primary language**: C++, Panda Assembly, ETS (.ets)

## About Tests

The **tests/** directory contains several compiler-relevant validation layers:

- **checked/** - main IR/codegen/disasm/events verification layer for compiler changes
- **benchmarks/** - micro-benchmarks
- **vm-benchmarks/** - broader benchmarking harness
- **irtoc-interpreter-tests/** - interpreter/irtoc validation suites
- **verifier-tests/** - verifier-focused coverage
- **regression/** - regression tests
- **tests-u-runner/** and **tests-u-runner-2/** - runner-based workflows used by CI and local repro
- **cts-assembly/** - assembly-based compatibility coverage
- **fuzztest/** - fuzz-based coverage

ETS-specific compiler validation also exists outside this directory under `plugins/ets/tests/compiler/`, `plugins/ets/tests/checked/`,
`plugins/ets/tests/ets_es_checked/`, and `plugins/ets/tests/jitinterface/`.

## Directory Structure

Main File Directories

```
tests/
├── CMakeLists.txt                   # Test build configuration
├── benchmarks/                      # Performance benchmarks
├── checked/                         # Compiler verification tests with checker DSL
├── irtoc-interpreter-tests/         # irtoc interpreter validation
├── regression/                      # Regression tests
├── tests-u-runner/                  # URunner v1
├── tests-u-runner-2/                # URunner v2
├── verifier-tests/                  # Verifier coverage
├── vm-benchmarks/                   # VM/compiler performance suites
└── ...
```

## Build Commands

See @../AGENTS.md

## Running Tests

### Common Compiler-Facing Targets

- `ninja core_checked_tests` - run checked tests
- `ninja irtoc-interpreter-tests` - run irtoc interpreter validation
- compiler gtests are defined under `compiler/` and described in `compiler/AGENTS.md`
- runtime/compiler boundary gtests live in `runtime/tests/stack_walker_test.cpp`, `runtime/tests/c2i_bridge_test.cpp`, and `runtime/tests/i2c_bridge_test.cpp`
- ETS compiler-facing targets include `ets_tests_compiler_optimizer`, `compiler_wrong_boot_context_test`, `ets_interop_js_gtests`, `ets_interop_js_checked_tests`, `ets_run_jit_ets_code_tests`, `ets_run_jit_osr_ets_code_tests`, and `ets_run_aot_ets_code_tests`

### irtoc Change Checklist

- For new or changed `irtoc` fastpaths or intrinsics, cover more than one layer
- Use `ninja irtoc_unit_tests` and `ninja irtoc_compiler_unit_tests` for DSL and backend validation
- Use `ninja irtoc-interpreter-tests` for generated interpreter or entrypoint behavior
- Use `ninja core_checked_tests` plus a focused checked repro such as `tests/checked/osr_irtoc_test.pa` for compiler or runtime interaction
- Run runtime bridge gtests when ABI, stack-frame, or boundary behavior changes
- Add ETS checked or execution coverage when the change is exposed through ETS intrinsics or stdlib APIs
- Do not claim an `irtoc` performance win without comparing it to the managed or C++ baseline on the relevant
  architecture and build mode; see the repo-level policy in `../AGENTS.md`

### Checked Tests

`tests/checked/` is one of the most important tools for compiler work. The checker DSL can validate:

- IR after a chosen pass via `METHOD`, `PASS_AFTER`, `INST`, `INST_NOT`, `IN_BLOCK`
- generated assembly/disassembly via `ASM_METHOD`, `ASM`, `ASM_NOT`
- compiler/runtime events via `EVENT`, `EVENT_NOT`
- JIT, AOT, LLVM AOT, frontend, and bytecode-optimizer workflows via `RUN`, `RUN_PAOC`, `RUN_AOT`, `RUN_LLVM`, `RUN_BCO`, `RUN_FRONTEND`
- advanced workflows also rely on `ASM_INST`, `INST_COUNT`, `IR_COUNT`, `CHECK_GROUP`, `DEFINE_FRONTEND_OPTIONS`, `RUN_PGO_PROF`, and `RUN_PGO_PAOC`

Read `tests/checked/README.md` before adding or debugging compiler checked tests.

Good repro seeds for compiler work:

- `tests/checked/disasm_and_log_demo.pa` - disasm dump + event/log workflow
- `tests/checked/deoptimize_compare.pa` - `DeoptimizeIf` / lowering checks
- `tests/checked/osr.pa` - OSR event validation
- `tests/checked/osr_irtoc_test.pa` - irtoc + OSR interaction

### Important Constraints

- most checked tests are skipped in `Release` unless the test explicitly sets `SUPPORT_RELEASE`
- many checked targets are gated by architecture or sanitizer configuration
- checked OSR cases are arm64-only
- `tests/CMakeLists.txt` also adds arm64-only OSR run variants to broader test suites
- `force_jit: true` expands to `--compiler-hotness-threshold=0 --no-async-jit=true --compiler-enable-jit=true`
- for runtime-side caveats of force-jit, interpreter defaults, or AOT interaction, use `../runtime/AGENTS.md`

### Runner-Based Reproduction

- `tests/tests-u-runner/readme.md` describes how to reproduce CI runs locally
- `tests/tests-u-runner-2/README.md` contains the newer URunner workflow and rerun instructions
- both runners cover configurations such as `INT`, `JIT`, `IRTOC`, `AOT`, and `LLVM`
- both runners can narrow a repro with `--test-file`, `--test-list`, and `--filter`
- for flaky investigation, rerun with `--verbose=all` first and extract the actual `ark` / `ark_aot` command before
  trying to parallelize or stress it
- URunner v1 timeout thread dumps still require `--handle-timeout` and `sudo`
- URunner2 still depends on the environment described in `.urunner.env`; missing mandatory variables are a first-class
  failure mode
- URunner2 repro blocks also include the exact per-step commands plus required environment variables, not only the
  top-level runner invocation
- ETS intrinsic/codegen regressions often live in URunner2 suites such as `compiler_checker_suite` and `ets-es-checked`
- representative ETS URunner2 repros include `plugins/ets/tests/checked_urunner/ets_tests/checks_elimination_osr.ets`,
  `plugins/ets/tests/checked_urunner/ets_tests/escape_deoptimization.ets`, and
  `plugins/ets/tests/checked_urunner/ets_tests/inlining_cache_events.ets`

### Flaky Debug Workflow

Use `../docs/flaky_debugging.md` as the maintained end-to-end workflow. From the tests side, the minimum discipline is:

- start with the exact CI repro line emitted by the current runner or checked target
- keep the same build, panda files, interpreter mode, and AOT/JIT shape
- extract the real `ark` / `ark_aot` command before deeper debugging
- add compiler dumps or GC logging only after the minimized repro still reproduces

### Performance Investigation

- `tests/benchmarks/` and `tests/vm-benchmarks/` are the main core benchmark suites
- `plugins/ets/tests/scripts/micro-benchmarks/README.md` covers ETS micro-benchmark runs across `int`, `jit`, and `aot`
- `runtime/tooling/sampler/docs/cpu_profiler.md` describes the sampling profiler / CPU profiler flow
- use `../compiler/docs/performance_workflows.md` for the compiler-side investigation loop rather than duplicating it here

## Documentation

- `../docs/flaky_debugging.md` - current checked/URunner/high-load/asm-to-IR repro workflow
- `../compiler/docs/performance_workflows.md` - current benchmark and performance workflow

## Code Style

See @../AGENTS.md

## Dependencies

- **Python 3**: For test scripts
- **Ruby**: Required by `tests/checked/checker.rb`
- **es2panda**: Compile .ets to .abc
- **ark**: Run .abc files
- **ark_aot**: AOT and LLVM AOT test workflows

content.thinking:
**Considering script documentation**

I'm looking at the `pr_surface.sh` script, thinking that I could list files and likely tests based on the revision range. I should implement some documentation to help users understand how to use these scripts. It might be useful to include examples with the actual build path, like `builds/static_core-fastverify/bin/es2panda`, while ensuring the skill is reusable for the project. Maybe I can also mention checking the repo build directory or allowing command-line arguments for flexibility.

**Designing a probe case**

I want to create a `probe_cases.py` that aims to batch compile candidate code snippets and classify the results. I need it to be generic, which is intriguing because I can consider various scenarios and how to categorize the outcomes. I wonder about different methods of compilation and classification — should I think about performance metrics or just basic functionality? This could open up several paths for organization, and I'm curious about the best approach to take!

**Exploring script development**

I’m thinking about a script called `pr_surface.sh`, which could take a revision range and maybe an optional repo path. The output would list changed files and tests using `git diff --name-status`. I wonder if I should keep it simple with plain text or use JSON, but maybe Python would be easier, especially since `jq` might not be available. I’m considering implementing it as a shell script, capturing changed files and relevant commit subjects using `git log --oneline`.

**Designing the PR script**

I’m thinking about how the script can handle both a merge commit hash and a range. If the user provides just one argument, I could use `${1}^!` to capture it. There’s also the option to implement shared documentation, like oracle.md and pr-triage.md, but I’m contemplating whether to include project-specific instructions. For example, directing the user to read from specific paths in the runtime repo. I wonder if it’s necessary to reference the top-level README for commands.

**Clarifying implementation instructions**

I’m considering saying something like, "Use the build of es2panda already available in your environment; a typical path in this workspace is...". It's important to implement the skills with clear and concise instructions. I do worry about ensuring that skill names in the frontmatter must exactly match their parent directory names, which includes ones like `ets-spec-gap-hunter` and `ets-pr-backfill`. It seems like getting this detail right will be crucial for everything to function smoothly!

**Evaluating file scripting methods**

I’m considering using `bash chmod +x` on scripts, but I also need to create directories with the write tool, which auto-creates parents. For multiple files, I'll likely use the write method instead of `bash cat >`. The idea of creating nine files comes to mind, and I think about utilizing multi_tool.parallel for independent writes. However, managing all these files methodically is key, so I’ll have to inspect the root path and determine location references in a strategic manner.

**Considering documentation references**

I’m thinking about including one document, `pr-triage.md`, in the ets-pr-backfill references, along with `mapping-pr-tests-to-spec.md`. The user asked for two skills, so it seems reasonable to have a couple of references. My plan could be to keep one reference in each skill plus some shared documents. This way, I’m ensuring clear and useful documentation without overwhelming the user. It’s all about balancing thoroughness with simplicity!

**Clarifying documentation steps**

I want to make the information concise but clear. For the shared deduplication document, my focus is on comparing semantics rather than the text itself and considering steps and examples that might involve type alias contexts. I've identified duplicate categories: exact, near, and unique.

In the shared harvesting document, I need to think about how to turn findings into ETS templates, keeping naming conventions, and ensuring we parametrize with the same rules. It’s okay if the current implementation fails too. I'll need to clarify specifics, especially regarding negative tests.

script documentation**\n\nI'm looking at the `pr_surface.sh` script, thinking that I could list files and likely tests based on the revision range. I should implement some documentation to help users understand how to use these scripts. It might be useful to include examples with the actual build path, like `builds/static_core-fastverify/bin/es2panda`, while ensuring the skill is reusable for the project. Maybe I can also mention checking the repo build directory or allowing command-line arguments for flexibility."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Designing a probe case**\n\nI want to create a `probe_cases.py` that aims to batch compile candidate code snippets and classify the results. I need it to be generic, which is intriguing because I can consider various scenarios and how to categorize the outcomes. I wonder about different methods of compilation and classification — should I think about performance metrics or just basic functionality? This could open up several paths for organization, and I'm curious about the best approach to take!"},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Exploring script development**\n\nI’m thinking about a script called `pr_surface.sh`, which could take a revision range and maybe an optional repo path. The output would list changed files and tests using `git diff --name-status`. I wonder if I should keep it simple with plain text or use JSON, but maybe Python would be easier, especially since `jq` might not be available. I’m considering implementing it as a shell script, capturing changed files and relevant commit subjects using `git log --oneline`."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Designing the PR script**\n\nI’m thinking about how the script can handle both a merge commit hash and a range. If the user provides just one argument, I could use `${1}^!` to capture it. There’s also the option to implement shared documentation, like oracle.md and pr-triage.md, but I’m contemplating whether to include project-specific instructions. For example, directing the user to read from specific paths in the runtime repo. I wonder if it’s necessary to reference the top-level README for commands."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Clarifying implementation instructions**\n\nI’m considering saying something like, \"Use the build of es2panda already available in your environment; a typical path in this workspace is...\". It's important to implement the skills with clear and concise instructions. I do worry about ensuring that skill names in the frontmatter must exactly match their parent directory names, which includes ones like `ets-spec-gap-hunter` and `ets-pr-backfill`. It seems like getting this detail right will be crucial for everything to function smoothly!"},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Evaluating file scripting methods**\n\nI’m considering using `bash chmod +x` on scripts, but I also need to create directories with the write tool, which auto-creates parents. For multiple files, I'll likely use the write method instead of `bash cat >`. The idea of creating nine files comes to mind, and I think about utilizing multi_tool.parallel for independent writes. However, managing all these files methodically is key, so I’ll have to inspect the root path and determine location references in a strategic manner."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Considering documentation references**\n\nI’m thinking about including one document, `pr-triage.md`, in the ets-pr-backfill references, along with `mapping-pr-tests-to-spec.md`. The user asked for two skills, so it seems reasonable to have a couple of references. My plan could be to keep one reference in each skill plus some shared documents. This way, I’m ensuring clear and useful documentation without overwhelming the user. It’s all about balancing thoroughness with simplicity!"},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Clarifying documentation steps**\n\nI want to make the information concise but clear. For the shared deduplication document, my focus is on comparing semantics rather than the text itself and considering steps and examples that might involve type alias contexts. I've identified duplicate categories: exact, near, and unique.\n\nIn the shared harvesting document, I need to think about how to turn findings into ETS templates, keeping naming conventions, and ensuring we parametrize with the same rules. It’s okay if the current implementation fails too. I'll need to clarify specifics, especially regarding negative tests."}]}

---
cases:
    - defs: |-
          type 123 = int;

    - defs: |-
          type .X = Long;

    - defs: |-
          type null = byte;

    - defs: |-
          type this = Object;

    - defs: |-
          type int = Int;

    - defs: |-
          interface Weight {}
          class Body implements Weight {}
          type Weight = Double;

    - defs: |-
          type self = this;

    - defs: |-
          class A<T, V> {}
          type Box<T> = A<Boolean, T>;
      use: |-
          let v: Box<Int> = new A<Int, Int>();

    - defs: |-
          type E = E

    - defs: |-
          type E = string | E

    - defs: |-
          type A<T> = Array<A>

    - defs: |-
          type A<T> = A<T>

    - defs: |-
          type A = C
          type B = A
          type C = B

    # recursive cases
    - defs: |-
          type RU = number | string | RU // TypeError: Circular type alias reference
      use: |-
          let v1: RU = 1.0
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0311: Circular type alias reference"

    - defs: |-
          type RF = (p: RF) => RF
      use: |-
          let v1: RF = (p: RF): RF => { return p }

    - defs: |-
        { class ClassNestedInBlock{} }
        type TAlias = ClassNestedInBlock;

    - defs: |-
          enum Axis { X, Y, Z };
          type Fields = Axis[];
      use: |-
          let v: Fields = new Axis[6];
          let ok = v.length == 6;

    - defs: |-
        type TStringAlias? = String | undefined

    - defs: |-
        @interface ClassAuthor {}
        type A = ClassAuthor

    - defs: |-
        @interface ClassAuthor {}
        type A = ClassAuthor | String

    - defs: |-
        type A = class MyClassDec {}

    - defs: |-
        class AliasMe {}
        type Alias = AliasMe<X> // CTE:  TypeError: Type 'AliasMe' is not generic.

    - defs: |-
        class AliasMe<X> {}
        type Alias = AliasMe<X> // CTE: TypeError: Cannot find type 'X'.

    - defs: |-
        type A<T, U> = T | A<U, T>[]
      use: |-
        let xx: A<Boolean, String> = new A<Boolean, String>()  //CTE: TypeError: The union type is not constructible.
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0300: The union type is not constructible."

    - defs: |-
        final class FinitaLaComedia {}
        type Alias<T extends FinitaLaComedia> = T
      use: |-
        let x: Alias<number> = '1'  //CTE: TypeError: Type '"1"' cannot be assigned to type 'Double'

    - defs: |-
        class FinitaLaComedia {}
        type Alias<T = FinitaLaComedia> = T
      use: |-
        let x: Alias<number> = '1'  //CTE: TypeError: Type '"1"' cannot be assigned to type 'Double'

    - defs: |-
        class FinitaLaComedia {}
        type Alias<T = FinitaLaComedia> = T
      use: |-
        let x: Alias = 1  //CTE: TypeError: Type 'Int' cannot be assigned to type 'FinitaLaComedia'

    - defs: |-
        type Alias<T extends string> = T

      use: |-
        let xx: Alias<Number> = 1
        let ok = xx == 1;

    - defs: |-
        final class FinitaLaComedia {}
        type Alias<T extends FinitaLaComedia> = T

      use: |-
        let xx: Alias<Number> = 1
        let ok = xx == 1;

    - defs: |-
        final class FinitaLaComedia {}
        type Alias<T extends FinitaLaComedia> = T

      use: |-
        let xx: Alias<String> = "Divine"
        let ok = xx instanceof String && xx instanceof Alias<String>;

    - defs: |-
        type TSStyleAlias<T> = { value: T } // SyntaxError: Using object literals to declare types in place is not supported
      use: |-
        let xx: TSStyleAlias<String>;
      expected_out: "Syntax error ESY177354: Using object literals to declare types in place is not supported. Please declare types and interfaces explicitly!"

    - defs: |-
        class MC {}
        type TSStyleAlias = MC | { } // SyntaxError: Using object literals to declare types in place is not supported
      use: |-
        let xx: TSStyleAlias
      expected_out: "Syntax error ESY177354: Using object literals to declare types in place is not supported. Please declare types and interfaces explicitly!"

    - defs: |-
        class MC {}
        type TSStyleAlias = MC | undefined | { } // SyntaxError: Using object literals to declare types in place is not supported
      use: |-
        let xx: TSStyleAlias
      expected_out: "Syntax error ESY177354: Using object literals to declare types in place is not supported. Please declare types and interfaces explicitly!"

    - defs: |-
        type IsString<T> = T extends string ? true : false; // TypeError: Conditional type aliases are not supported.
        type A = IsString<string>; // true
        type B = IsString<number>; // false
      use: |-
        let xx: IsString<string>;
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE136365: Conditional type aliases are not supported. Introduce a new type with constraints explicitly, or rewrite logic using Object!"

    - defs: |-
        interface Named { name: string; }        
        interface Aged { age: number; }
        type Person = Named & Aged // SyntaxError: Intersection types are not supported
      use: |-
        let xx: Person;
      expected_out: "Syntax error ESY145527: Intersection types are not supported, use inheritance instead!"

    - defs: |-
        type CustomReturnType<T> = T extends (...args: Any[]) => infer P ? P : Any // TypeError: Conditional type aliases are not supported
      use: |-
        let xx: CustomReturnType<Long>;
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE136365: Conditional type aliases are not supported. Introduce a new type with constraints explicitly, or rewrite logic using Object!"

    - defs: |-
        type CArrayType<T> = T extends (infer Item)[] ? Item : T  // TypeError: Conditional type aliases are not supported
      use: |-
        let xx: CArrayType<Long>;
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE136365: Conditional type aliases are not supported. Introduce a new type with constraints explicitly, or rewrite logic using Object!"

    - defs: |-
        type CCT = [string, string, string?]  // TypeError: Conditional type aliases are not supported
      use: |-
        let xx: CCT
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE136365: Conditional type aliases are not supported. Introduce a new type with constraints explicitly, or rewrite logic using Object!"

    - defs: |-
        type Rec<T extends string | number> = T | Rec<T>  // TypeError: Circular type alias reference
      use: |-
        let xx: Rec<String>
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0311: Circular type alias reference"

    - defs: |-
        type NESA<X> = [X, ...X[]] // Syntax Error: Invalid Type
      use: |-
        let xx: NESA<String>

        type ELP = | string  // Syntax Error: Invalid Type

cases:
  - desc: |-
      >-
        Enumeration type enum specifies a distinct user-defined type
        with an associated set of named readonly members that define its
        possible enumeration values.
        If initializers for all members are omitted, then the first member
        is assigned the value zero. The other member is assigned the value
        of the immediately preceding member plus one.
        params: all enum member values are set implicitly
    check_variants:
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
          enum Color2 { Red, Green, Blue }
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Color { Red, Green, Blue, }
          enum Color2 { Red, Green, Blue, }
    decl: |-
      [decl_data]
    sub_cases_logic:
      - |-
        let r: Color = Color.Red
        let g: Color = Color.Green
        let b: Color = Color.Blue
        arktest.assertTrue(r instanceof Color)
        arktest.assertTrue(g instanceof Color)
        arktest.assertTrue(b instanceof Color)
        arktest.assertFalse(r instanceof Color2)
        arktest.assertFalse(g instanceof Color2)
        arktest.assertFalse(b instanceof Color2)
        arktest.assertTrue(r.valueOf() instanceof int)
        arktest.assertTrue(g.valueOf() instanceof int)
        arktest.assertTrue(b.valueOf() instanceof int)
        arktest.assertEQ(r, 0)
        arktest.assertEQ(g, 1)
        arktest.assertEQ(b, 2)
      - |-
        let r = Color.Red
        let g = Color.Green
        let b = Color.Blue
        arktest.assertTrue(r instanceof Color)
        arktest.assertTrue(g instanceof Color)
        arktest.assertTrue(b instanceof Color)
        arktest.assertFalse(r instanceof Color2)
        arktest.assertFalse(g instanceof Color2)
        arktest.assertFalse(b instanceof Color2)
        arktest.assertTrue(r.valueOf() instanceof int)
        arktest.assertTrue(g.valueOf() instanceof int)
        arktest.assertTrue(b.valueOf() instanceof int)
        arktest.assertEQ(r, 0)
        arktest.assertEQ(g, 1)
        arktest.assertEQ(b, 2)
      - |-
        arktest.assertTrue(Color.Red instanceof Color)
        arktest.assertTrue(Color.Green instanceof Color)
        arktest.assertTrue(Color.Blue instanceof Color)
        arktest.assertFalse(Color.Red instanceof Color2)
        arktest.assertFalse(Color.Green instanceof Color2)
        arktest.assertFalse(Color.Blue instanceof Color2)
        arktest.assertTrue(Color.Red.valueOf() instanceof int)
        arktest.assertTrue(Color.Green.valueOf() instanceof int)
        arktest.assertTrue(Color.Blue.valueOf() instanceof int)
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Red, 0)
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Green, 1)
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Blue, 2)
    test_suffix: "aemvasi"
    ids: "0-5"

  - desc: |-
      >-
        Enumeration type enum specifies a distinct user-defined type
        with an associated set of named readonly members that define its
        possible enumeration values.
        params: modifying enum variable by other named constant
    check_variants:
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
    decl: |-
      [decl_data]
    sub_cases_logic:
      - |-
        let r: Color = Color.Red
        r = Color.Blue
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Red, 0)
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Green, 1)
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Blue, 2)
        arktest.assertEQ(r, 2)
      - |-
        let g: Color = Color.Green
        g = Color.Blue
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Red, 0)
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Green, 1)
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Blue, 2)
        arktest.assertEQ(g, 2)
      - |-
        let b: Color = Color.Blue
        b = Color.Red
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Red, 0)
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Green, 1)
        arktest.assertEQ(Color.Blue, 2)
        arktest.assertEQ(b, 0)
    test_suffix: "mevbonc"
    ids: "6-8"

  - desc: |-
      >-
        Qualification by type name is mandatory to access
        the enumeration member, except initializer expression
        params: accessing without qualification by type name
    check_variants:
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Flags { Read = 3, Write = 5, ReadWrite = Read | Write }
        check_val1: "3"
        check_val2: "5"
        check_val3: "7"
        check_type: "int"
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Flags: long { Read = 3, Write = 5, ReadWrite = Read | Write }
        check_val1: "3"
        check_val2: "5"
        check_val3: "7"
        check_type: "long"
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Flags: int { Read = 3, Write = 5, ReadWrite = Read | Write }
        check_val1: "3"
        check_val2: "5"
        check_val3: "7"
        check_type: "int"
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Flags: short { Read = 3, Write = 5, ReadWrite = Read | Write }
        check_val1: "3"
        check_val2: "5"
        check_val3: "7"
        check_type: "short"
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Flags: byte { Read = 3, Write = 5, ReadWrite = Read | Write }
        check_val1: "3"
        check_val2: "5"
        check_val3: "7"
        check_type: "byte"
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Flags: double { Read = 5.5, Write = 72.3, ReadWrite = Read + Write }
        check_val1: "5.5"
        check_val2: "72.3"
        check_val3: "77.8"
        check_type: "double"
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Flags: float { Read = 5.5f, Write = 72.3f, ReadWrite = Read + Write }
        check_val1: "5.5f"
        check_val2: "72.3f"
        check_val3: "77.8f"
        check_type: "float"
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Flags { Read = "abc", Write = "efg", ReadWrite = Read + Write }
        check_val1: "\"abc\""
        check_val2: "\"efg\""
        check_val3: "\"abcefg\""
        check_type: "string"
      - decl_data: |-
          enum Flags: string { Read = "abc", Write = "efg", ReadWrite = Read + Write }
        check_val1: "\"abc\""
        check_val2: "\"efg\""
        check_val3: "\"abcefg\""
        check_type: "string"
    decl: |-
      [decl_data]
    logic: |-
      let r: Flags = Flags.Read
      let w: Flags = Flags.Write
      let rw: Flags = Flags.ReadWrite
      arktest.assertTrue(r instanceof Flags)
      arktest.assertTrue(w instanceof Flags)
      arktest.assertTrue(rw instanceof Flags)
      arktest.assertTrue(r.valueOf() instanceof [check_type])
      arktest.assertTrue(w.valueOf() instanceof [check_type])
      arktest.assertTrue(rw.valueOf() instanceof [check_type])
      arktest.assertEQ(r, [check_val1])
      arktest.assertEQ(w, [check_val2])
      arktest.assertEQ(rw, [check_val3])
    test_suffix: "awqbtn"
    ids: "9-17"

  - desc: |-
      >-
        An enumeration member has a value that can be set explicitly
        by an initializer expression or implicitly.
        Each enumeration has a enumeration base type.
        A base type can be set explicitly (see Enumeration with Explicit Base
        Type) or inferred from initializers as follows:
        • If at least one member does not have an initializer,
        the inferred type is int. Types of all initializers must be are
        assignable to type int (see Assignability). Otherwise, a compile-time
        error occurs.
        • If types of all initializer expressions are assignable to type int
        then the inferred type is int;
        • If types of all initializer expressions are assignable to type string
        then the inferred type is string;
        • Otherwise, a compile-time error occurs.
        params: setting enum member values explicitly by const variable
    check_variants:
      - decl_data: |-
          const c: int = 5
          const check_val: int = c
          enum IntEnum { A = 5 }
      - decl_data: |-
          const c: short = 5
          const check_val: int = c
